topics.title: Text
topics.summary: Displays one or more lines of read-only text.

blocks.textContent: Text is the foundation of textual UI in SwiftUI. It is lightweight, declarative, and designed to render read-only content using typography that already feels native on each Apple platform.

blocks.code: Text("Hamlet")
blocks.title: Basic Text

blocks.textContent: By default, Text uses the platform body style, but that is usually just the starting point. In real interfaces, you often move between semantic styles like title, headline, caption, or a custom system font to establish hierarchy.

blocks.code:
Text("Hamlet")
    .font(.title)

Text("by William Shakespeare")
    .font(.system(size: 12, weight: .light, design: .serif))
    .italic()
blocks.title: Changing Font and Tone

blocks.textContent: Most day-to-day styling is done with modifiers. You can combine font, weight, design, color, underline, strikethrough, monospacing, and spacing-related modifiers to shape how text reads and feels.

blocks.code:
Text("Build once, adapt everywhere")
    .font(.headline)
    .foregroundStyle(.blue)
    .underline()
    .kerning(0.4)
blocks.title: Common Styling Modifiers

blocks.textContent: When only part of a sentence needs special emphasis, AttributedString is a better fit than stacking multiple views. It lets you keep the content together while styling specific ranges, and it works especially well with Markdown-based input.

blocks.title: Text with AttributedString

blocks.textContent: Text always asks for the space it needs, but layout constraints still matter. Once you place it inside a frame or a tighter container, SwiftUI may wrap, tighten, scale, or truncate the content depending on the modifiers you apply.

blocks.code:
Text("To be, or not to be, that is the question:")
    .frame(width: 100)

Text("Brevity is the soul of wit.")
    .frame(width: 100)
    .lineLimit(1)
blocks.title: Wrapping and Truncation

blocks.textContent: If a design is space-sensitive, lineLimit, truncationMode, minimumScaleFactor, allowsTightening, and textScale help you decide how aggressive Text should be when space runs out. This is one of the most practical parts of working with text in SwiftUI.

String literals passed directly to Text are treated as localization keys. That is convenient, but it also means you should be intentional: use verbatim when you want raw text, and prefer interpolation when building user-facing sentences so localization keeps working correctly across languages.

blocks.code:
Text("pencil")
Text(verbatim: "pencil")

let name = "Thiago"
Text("Hello, \(name)")
blocks.title: Localization and Verbatim Text

blocks.textContent: Interpolation is more powerful than just injecting strings. You can interpolate styled Text, formatted values, dates, and even images while keeping the sentence structure localizable.

blocks.code:
let name = Text("Thiago").bold()

Text("Hello, \(name)")
blocks.title: Interpolating Styled Text

blocks.textContent: Text also participates directly in accessibility. Beyond visible styling, you can mark headings, provide clearer labels, and refine how assistive technologies interpret or speak the content.

topics.title: Label
topics.summary: A standard label that combines a title with an icon.

blocks.textContent: Label is SwiftUI's built-in way to present text and icon together as a single semantic component. It is cleaner than manually pairing Image and Text when the intent is clearly 'icon plus title'.

blocks.code: Label("Favorites", systemImage: "star")
blocks.title: Basic Label

blocks.textContent: The main advantage of Label is intent. Instead of building a visual arrangement by hand, you describe a labeled UI element and let SwiftUI adapt it correctly for buttons, lists, toolbars, menus, and accessibility.

You can create Label using simple string and symbol parameters, or with title and icon closures when the content needs more control. That makes it flexible without losing the semantic meaning of the component.

blocks.title: Label with Custom Closures

blocks.textContent: Label becomes especially useful when combined with labelStyle. Depending on the context, you may want to show both icon and title, only the icon, or only the title. This is common in compact layouts, sidebars, and toolbars.

blocks.code:
VStack(spacing: 16) {
    Label("Profile", systemImage: "person.crop.circle")
        .labelStyle(.titleAndIcon)

    Label("Profile", systemImage: "person.crop.circle")
        .labelStyle(.iconOnly)

    Label("Profile", systemImage: "person.crop.circle")
        .labelStyle(.titleOnly)
}
blocks.title: Using Label Styles

blocks.textContent: Label appears naturally inside Button, Menu, and navigation surfaces because it gives you a compact, readable control with almost no extra effort. It is one of the easiest ways to make actions feel polished.

blocks.title: Label Inside a Button

blocks.textContent: Because Label models title and icon together, it usually produces better semantics than manually assembling the same structure. In many cases, that means less accessibility work and more consistent behavior across the system.

topics.title: LocalizedStringKey
topics.summary: A key type SwiftUI uses to resolve localizable text.

blocks.textContent: LocalizedStringKey is part of the reason Text feels localization-friendly by default. When you pass a string literal into Text, SwiftUI treats it as a localization key instead of just raw text.

This distinction matters because string literals and string variables do not behave the same way. A literal can participate in SwiftUI's localization pipeline automatically, while a regular String value usually does not unless you convert it explicitly.

blocks.title: Using a Localization Key

blocks.textContent: A common source of confusion is assuming that Text(someString) localizes everything automatically. If someString is a String variable, SwiftUI treats it as plain text. If you want localization behavior, LocalizedStringKey is the bridge.

blocks.code:
let writingImplement = "pencil"

Text(writingImplement)
Text(LocalizedStringKey(writingImplement))
blocks.title: String Versus LocalizedStringKey

blocks.textContent: LocalizedStringKey also works with interpolation, which is one of the safest ways to build user-facing text. It keeps the sentence structure localizable instead of forcing every language into the same hard-coded word order.

blocks.code:
let name = "Thiago"
Text("Hello, \(name)")
blocks.title: Localized Interpolation

topics.title: Image
topics.summary: Displays an image from assets, symbols, platform images, or other image sources.

blocks.textContent: Image is SwiftUI's view for presenting visual assets. In practice, it is used for app illustrations, thumbnails, icons, SF Symbols, and also bridged images coming from UIKit, AppKit, or lower-level drawing APIs.

You can create an Image from your asset catalog, from SF Symbols, from UIImage or NSImage, and even from CGImage. That flexibility matters because image data in real apps rarely comes from only one place.

blocks.code:
Image("Landscape_4")

Image(systemName: "bolt.fill")
blocks.title: Asset Image and SF Symbol

blocks.textContent: A common mistake is expecting Image to automatically resize like a flexible layout view. By default, it renders at its natural size. If the design needs scaling, resizable is usually the first modifier you reach for.

blocks.code:
Image("Landscape_4")
    .resizable()
    .aspectRatio(contentMode: .fit)
blocks.title: Resizable Image

blocks.textContent: Once an image becomes resizable, aspect ratio becomes important. Using fit preserves the entire image inside the available space, while fill prioritizes covering the container, which often means part of the image gets clipped.

blocks.code:
Image("Landscape_4")
    .resizable()
    .aspectRatio(contentMode: .fit)

Image("Landscape_4")
    .resizable()
    .aspectRatio(contentMode: .fill)
    .frame(width: 180, height: 120)
    .clipped()
blocks.title: Fit Versus Fill

blocks.textContent: For icons, SF Symbols are often a better choice than static assets. They align naturally with text, inherit tint and font-driven sizing, and support symbol-specific rendering behaviors like palette and hierarchical modes.

blocks.code:
Image(systemName: "heart.fill")
    .symbolRenderingMode(.hierarchical)
    .foregroundStyle(.pink)
    .font(.title2)
blocks.title: Styling an SF Symbol

blocks.textContent: Image also gives you control over rendering behavior. Interpolation, antialiasing, template rendering, and symbol rendering mode can change how sharp, tintable, or stylized an image appears in the final interface.
When working with platform APIs, it is common to bridge existing image objects into SwiftUI. That makes Image an important integration point, not just a view for bundle assets.

blocks.code:
let uiImage = UIImage(named: "avatar")!

Image(uiImage: uiImage)
    .resizable()
    .scaledToFit()
blocks.title: Creating Image from UIImage

blocks.textContent: Accessibility is easy to overlook with images. If an image carries meaning or works as a control, it should have a useful label. If it is purely decorative, mark it as decorative so assistive technologies can safely ignore it.

blocks.code:
Image(systemName: "trash", label: Text("Delete"))

Image(decorative: "background-texture")
blocks.title: Meaningful and Decorative Images

blocks.textContent: A useful mental model is that Image is not the final bitmap itself, but a view value that SwiftUI resolves later in the rendering environment. That is why environment-driven behavior like tinting, scaling, or symbol rendering can affect the final result.

topics.title: AsyncImage
topics.summary: Asynchronously loads and displays an image from a URL.

blocks.textContent: AsyncImage is SwiftUI's built-in way to fetch and display remote images. It is convenient for simple cases where you want a URL-backed image without building your own loader or caching pipeline first.
At its simplest, you pass a URL and SwiftUI handles the request using the shared URLSession. While the image is loading, the view shows a default placeholder that occupies the available space.

blocks.code:
AsyncImage(url: URL(string: "https://example.com/icon.png"))
    .frame(width: 200, height: 200)
blocks.title: Basic AsyncImage

blocks.textContent: In real interfaces, the default placeholder is often not enough. A custom placeholder gives the screen better visual stability and makes the loading state feel intentional instead of temporary.

blocks.title: Custom Placeholder

blocks.textContent: One important detail is that image-specific modifiers like resizable do not belong on AsyncImage itself. They need to be applied to the Image value you receive inside the content closure.
If you need more control, use the phase-based initializer. AsyncImagePhase lets you react to loading, success, and failure explicitly, which is usually the better choice for production UI.

blocks.title: Handling AsyncImagePhase

blocks.textContent: AsyncImage is great for straightforward remote images, but it is intentionally simple. If your app needs aggressive caching, retry policies, request headers, progressive loading, or image transformations, you will usually outgrow it and move to a custom pipeline or a dedicated image library.

Just like Image, AsyncImage still participates in layout through the views you return. That means frame, clipping, aspect ratio, and placeholder sizing all affect whether the component feels polished or visually jumpy while content loads.

topics.title: AttributedString
topics.summary: A value type for rich text with inline attributes and styling.

blocks.textContent: AttributedString lets you style portions of text without breaking a sentence into several separate Text views. It is Swift's value-type model for rich text, and it works naturally with SwiftUI.

It becomes useful when only specific ranges need emphasis, links, colors, weights, or semantic attributes. Instead of composing everything manually, you keep the content together and apply formatting where it belongs.

blocks.title: Basic AttributedString

blocks.textContent: One of the nicest features is Markdown parsing. That makes AttributedString especially good for app content, documentation, onboarding text, or any place where styled text needs to stay readable in source form.

blocks.title: AttributedString from Markdown

blocks.textContent: When AttributedString is rendered inside Text, inline attributes can coexist with SwiftUI modifiers. In general, the string's own attributes are the more specific source of truth, which makes them ideal for partial styling.

In practice, AttributedString is the right tool when the text is still one sentence or one paragraph, but some parts need more visual or semantic weight. If the content is structurally different, separate Text views are often still clearer.

topics.title: Button
topics.summary: A control that performs an action when triggered.

blocks.textContent: Button is the standard SwiftUI control for triggering actions. It wraps intent and presentation together, so instead of wiring touch events manually, you describe what should happen and let SwiftUI adapt the interaction to the current platform.

blocks.title: Basic Button

blocks.textContent: In practice, Button becomes more useful when its label carries visual context. You can pass a text title for simple actions, but custom labels are common when you need iconography, hierarchy, or a more expressive layout.

blocks.title: Button with Label

blocks.textContent: Buttons inherit a lot from context, but buttonStyle lets you push the control toward a specific role or emphasis. This is where you decide whether the action should feel bordered, prominent, plain, or closer to the surrounding content.

blocks.title: Common Button Styles

blocks.textContent: Roles help SwiftUI communicate meaning beyond appearance. A destructive button can automatically adopt stronger visual treatment in alerts, confirmation dialogs, and menus, which keeps the interface semantically consistent without extra work.

blocks.title: Destructive Role

blocks.textContent: A Button is still just a view, so it participates naturally in state-driven UI. Disabling, tinting, resizing, or changing the label based on app state is usually more important than custom drawing, because the quality of a button often comes from clear feedback rather than decoration.

blocks.title: Button Driven by State

topics.title: Toggle
topics.summary: A control that toggles between on and off states.

blocks.textContent: Toggle represents a binary choice backed by mutable state. In SwiftUI, it is usually bound directly to a Boolean source of truth, which makes it a clean example of the framework's data flow model: the control updates state, and the interface reacts automatically.

blocks.code:
@State private var isEnabled = true

Toggle("Airplane Mode", isOn: $isEnabled)
blocks.title: Basic Toggle

blocks.textContent: The binding is the important part. Toggle is not designed to own state by itself, so its behavior stays predictable when the value comes from @State, @Binding, or an observable model. That keeps the control simple and makes it fit naturally into forms and settings screens.

Like many SwiftUI controls, Toggle can use a plain text title or a custom label view. A richer label is useful when the setting needs context, such as an icon, secondary tone, or stronger hierarchy in a list.

blocks.title: Toggle with Custom Label

blocks.textContent: On iOS, Toggle defaults to the familiar switch appearance, but ToggleStyle gives you room to change that when the interface calls for a different treatment. Button-like toggles, checkbox styles on other platforms, and custom designs all build on the same on/off state model.

blocks.code:
Toggle("Favorite", isOn: $isFavorite)
    .toggleStyle(.button)
blocks.title: Alternative Toggle Style

blocks.textContent: When a toggle changes something beyond local UI, avoid placing too much side-effect logic inside the label or view body. It is usually cleaner to react to state changes with onChange or inside the owning model, so the control remains declarative and easy to reason about.

blocks.code:
Toggle("Use Cellular Data", isOn: $useCellular)
    .onChange(of: useCellular) { _, newValue in
        updateNetworkingPreference(newValue)
    }
blocks.title: Reacting to Changes

topics.title: Slider
topics.summary: A control for selecting a value from a bounded linear range of values.

blocks.textContent: Slider lets people choose a value by dragging a thumb along a track. It works best for continuous or near-continuous values such as volume, opacity, progress tuning, or any setting where relative adjustment matters more than exact numeric entry.

blocks.code:
@State private var volume = 0.5

Slider(value: $volume)
blocks.title: Basic Slider

blocks.textContent: Most real sliders should declare an explicit range. That communicates the minimum and maximum allowed values clearly in code, and it makes the control behave predictably when the backing value changes from elsewhere in the app.

blocks.code:
@State private var brightness = 0.8

Slider(value: $brightness, in: 0...1)
blocks.title: Slider with Range

blocks.textContent: Even though Slider feels continuous, it can step through fixed increments when you need more controlled input. This is useful when the value maps to known intervals, such as font size presets, playback speed, or rounded percentages.

blocks.code:
@State private var speed = 1.0

Slider(value: $speed, in: 0.5...2.0, step: 0.25)
blocks.title: Slider with Step

blocks.textContent: Labels matter more than they first appear. A slider without context is just a moving thumb, so surrounding it with a title, current value, or minimum and maximum cues makes the control far easier to understand and more accessible.

blocks.title: Slider with Labels

blocks.textContent: If changing the value triggers expensive work, prefer reacting when editing ends instead of on every tiny movement. That distinction keeps the interface responsive and avoids doing unnecessary updates while the user is still dragging.

blocks.title: Responding When Drag Ends

topics.title: Stepper
topics.summary: A control used to perform discrete increment and decrement tasks.

blocks.textContent: Stepper is a compact control for adjusting values in small, discrete steps. It works best when the user needs precision with predictable increments, such as item quantity, font size, guest count, or any setting where moving one step at a time is clearer than dragging a slider.

blocks.code:
@State private var quantity = 1

Stepper("Quantity: \(quantity)", value: $quantity)
blocks.title: Basic Stepper

blocks.textContent: Most steppers should define a range so the control communicates clear limits and avoids invalid values. This is especially useful in forms and configuration screens, where the allowed bounds are part of the domain, not just a UI detail.

blocks.code:
@State private var guests = 2

Stepper("Guests: \(guests)", value: $guests, in: 1...10)
blocks.title: Stepper with Range

blocks.textContent: You can also control the step size when each tap should move by more than one unit. That is useful for values that naturally change in larger intervals, such as percentages, minutes, or grouped counts.

blocks.code:
@State private var fontSize = 12

Stepper("Font Size: \(fontSize)", value: $fontSize, in: 8...32, step: 2)
blocks.title: Custom Step Size

blocks.textContent: There is also an action-based form of Stepper that lets you handle increment and decrement manually. That version is useful when the value is derived, validated somewhere else, or cannot be expressed as a simple writable binding.

blocks.title: Manual Increment and Decrement

topics.title: Picker
topics.summary: A control for selecting from a set of mutually exclusive values.

blocks.textContent: Picker presents a list of mutually exclusive choices backed by a selection value. The exact appearance depends heavily on context and style, which is one of its strengths: the same semantic control can feel native in forms, segmented interfaces, menus, and wheel-based selection.

blocks.title: Basic Picker

blocks.textContent: The selection binding and the tags inside the picker need to match. That detail is easy to miss at first, but it is fundamental: Picker decides which option is selected by comparing the bound value with each option's tag.

PickerStyle changes how choices are presented without changing the underlying selection model. On iOS, segmented, menu, inline, navigation link, and wheel-style presentations all serve different use cases, so the best choice depends more on UX intent than on code convenience.

blocks.title: Segmented Picker

blocks.textContent: Pickers become more maintainable when the options come from a real model type instead of loose strings. Enums that conform to CaseIterable and Identifiable often make selection code easier to read and harder to break as the app grows.

blocks.title: Picker Backed by an Enum

topics.title: DatePicker
topics.summary: A control for selecting an absolute date.

blocks.textContent: DatePicker lets people choose dates and times using controls that adapt to platform conventions. It is one of the clearest examples of SwiftUI wrapping a fairly complex system interaction into a simple binding-driven API.

blocks.code:
@State private var dueDate = Date()

DatePicker("Due Date", selection: $dueDate)
blocks.title: Basic DatePicker

blocks.textContent: The displayed components matter because they define the user's mental model. Sometimes you need only a date, sometimes only a time, and sometimes both. Being explicit keeps the control focused and avoids presenting unnecessary UI.

blocks.code:
DatePicker(
    "Reminder",
    selection: $reminderDate,
    displayedComponents: [.date, .hourAndMinute]
)
blocks.title: Date and Time Components

blocks.textContent: Constraining the selectable range is often essential. Booking flows, reminders, scheduling, and deadlines usually have valid windows, and putting those rules directly into DatePicker prevents impossible selections before they become validation errors.

blocks.code:
DatePicker(
    "Start Date",
    selection: $startDate,
    in: Date()...,
    displayedComponents: .date
)
blocks.title: Restricting the Date Range

blocks.textContent: Style changes the presentation significantly. Compact, wheel, and graphical styles each suggest different levels of space, precision, and context, so the right choice depends on whether the control should stay lightweight or become a more central part of the screen.

blocks.code:
DatePicker("Birthday", selection: $birthday, displayedComponents: .date)
    .datePickerStyle(.graphical)
blocks.title: Graphical DatePicker

topics.title: ColorPicker
topics.summary: A control that provides an affordance for selecting a color.

blocks.textContent: ColorPicker provides a native way to let people choose colors without building a custom palette from scratch. It is a good fit for profile customization, drawing tools, themes, tags, and any interface where color is user-editable state.

blocks.code:
@State private var selectedColor: Color = .blue

ColorPicker("Accent Color", selection: $selectedColor)
blocks.title: Basic ColorPicker

blocks.textContent: Like other input controls in SwiftUI, ColorPicker is driven by a binding. That keeps it easy to connect with previews, state restoration, and live interface updates because the chosen color is just another piece of app state.

Opacity support is optional. In some experiences, alpha is a meaningful part of the choice, but in others it adds noise or introduces edge cases. The control lets you decide whether transparency should be part of the editing surface.

blocks.code:
ColorPicker(
    "Overlay Color",
    selection: $overlayColor,
    supportsOpacity: false
)
blocks.title: Disabling Opacity Editing

blocks.textContent: A color picker works better when the interface also shows the chosen result in context. Pairing it with a shape, swatch, or live preview usually makes the experience clearer than exposing the control alone.

blocks.title: ColorPicker with Live Preview

topics.title: TextField
topics.summary: A control that displays an editable text interface.

blocks.textContent: TextField is the basic single-line text input control in SwiftUI. It is central to forms, authentication flows, search interfaces, and lightweight editing tasks where the user needs to enter or revise a short piece of text.

blocks.code:
@State private var username = ""

TextField("Username", text: $username)
blocks.title: Basic TextField

blocks.textContent: The prompt should tell the user what to enter, not restate the field's title vaguely. Clear placeholder and label choices matter because text inputs fail fast when the user is unsure what format or value is expected.

TextField can also bind to formatted values instead of raw strings. That becomes useful for numbers, currency, and other typed input, especially when you want SwiftUI to handle parsing and presentation consistently.

blocks.code:
@State private var amount = 0.0

TextField("Amount", value: $amount, format: .currency(code: "USD"))
blocks.title: TextField with a Formatted Value

blocks.textContent: Input behavior usually matters as much as the binding itself. Keyboard type, text input autocapitalization, submit labels, and autocorrection settings help shape the user's flow and reduce friction in real forms.

blocks.code:
TextField("Email", text: $email)
    .keyboardType(.emailAddress)
    .textInputAutocapitalization(.never)
    .autocorrectionDisabled()
    .submitLabel(.done)
blocks.title: Configuring Text Input Behavior

blocks.textContent: Focus management becomes important once a screen has more than one field. Pairing TextField with FocusState lets you move predictably between inputs, dismiss the keyboard, and keep the form behavior under your control.

blocks.title: Managing Focus

topics.title: SecureField
topics.summary: A control into which people securely enter private text.

blocks.textContent: SecureField is SwiftUI's single-line input control for sensitive text such as passwords, passcodes, and secret tokens. It behaves much like TextField, but it hides the entered content to protect privacy during input.

blocks.code:
@State private var password = ""

SecureField("Password", text: $password)
blocks.title: Basic SecureField

blocks.textContent: The main distinction from TextField is intent. When the content is sensitive, SecureField communicates that clearly to both the user and the system, which is better than manually masking text in a regular field.
SecureField is often used in authentication flows, so submit behavior matters. Pairing it with submitLabel and onSubmit usually makes the password step feel more deliberate and reduces extra taps.

blocks.title: Handling Submission

blocks.textContent: In forms with multiple inputs, SecureField works best when integrated with focus management. That keeps the login flow predictable and lets you move naturally from email or username into the secure field.

blocks.title: SecureField with FocusState

topics.title: TextEditor
topics.summary: A view that can display and edit long-form text.

blocks.textContent: TextEditor is SwiftUI's multiline text input view. It is the right choice when the user needs to write or edit larger amounts of text, such as notes, descriptions, comments, or draft content that goes beyond a single line.

blocks.code:
@State private var notes = ""

TextEditor(text: $notes)
blocks.title: Basic TextEditor

blocks.textContent: The practical difference from TextField is scope. TextField is optimized for short, single-line input, while TextEditor is designed for freeform writing where line breaks, scrolling, and extended editing are expected.

TextEditor is intentionally plain by default, so surrounding layout usually carries the visual structure. In real apps, it is common to place it inside a padded container, define a minimum height, and give the editing area a clearer boundary.

blocks.code:
TextEditor(text: $bio)
    .frame(minHeight: 160)
    .padding(8)
    .overlay {
        RoundedRectangle(cornerRadius: 12)
            .stroke(.quaternary)
    }
blocks.title: TextEditor with Visual Framing

blocks.textContent: Unlike TextField, TextEditor does not provide a built-in placeholder. If your UX depends on one, you usually layer a prompt-like Text view behind or above the editor and hide it once the user has typed content.

blocks.title: Custom Placeholder Pattern

topics.title: Menu
topics.summary: A control for presenting a menu of actions.

blocks.textContent: Menu presents a set of actions or choices from a compact trigger view. It is useful when you want to keep secondary actions available without giving each one permanent space in the main interface.

blocks.title: Basic Menu

blocks.textContent: Menu is best when the actions are real but not primary. If users need to trigger something often, hiding it inside a menu can add friction. For overflow actions, though, it keeps the screen cleaner without losing capability.
The trigger view can be simple text, an icon, or a custom label. A Label-based trigger often reads well because it suggests both the action surface and the kind of content hidden behind it.

blocks.title: Menu with a Custom Label

blocks.textContent: Menus can also group related content using nested menus, toggles, or pickers. That makes them surprisingly flexible, but the structure still needs to stay shallow enough to remain fast to scan and use.

blocks.title: Embedding a Picker in a Menu

topics.title: ProgressView
topics.summary: A view that shows the progress toward completion of a task.

blocks.textContent: ProgressView communicates ongoing work or measurable completion. It can represent an indeterminate operation, where the system only knows that work is happening, or a determinate one, where a current value can be compared against a total.

blocks.code: ProgressView()
blocks.title: Indeterminate ProgressView

blocks.textContent: When you have real progress data, passing a value and total gives users a much better sense of pace and expectation. That is usually worth doing whenever the task takes more than a moment and the app can measure completion reliably.

blocks.code: ProgressView(value: downloadProgress, total: 1.0)
blocks.title: Determinate Progress

blocks.textContent: Labels are often the difference between a spinner and useful feedback. A short title or current task description gives context, especially when loading is not instantaneous or when the user needs to trust that the app is still making progress.

blocks.title: ProgressView with Labels

blocks.textContent: ProgressViewStyle can change the visual treatment, but the more important design choice is usually whether progress should feel inline, blocking, or secondary. The control should match the importance of the operation it represents.

topics.title: Gauge
topics.summary: A view that shows a value within a range.

blocks.textContent: Gauge presents the current value of a bounded range using a more expressive visual metaphor than a plain progress indicator. It works well when the UI should communicate status, intensity, or level rather than just task completion.

blocks.title: Basic Gauge

blocks.textContent: The key difference from ProgressView is intent. ProgressView usually answers 'how much of this task is done?', while Gauge answers 'where does this current value sit within a known range?'. That distinction matters when choosing the more semantically correct component.
Gauge supports labels for the main title as well as minimum, maximum, and current value. Those additions make the component much clearer when the range or meaning is not obvious from context alone.

blocks.title: Gauge with Labels

blocks.textContent: GaugeStyle changes the visual personality of the component significantly. Depending on the platform and the context, a linear, accessory, or capacity-style gauge can feel more appropriate than the default presentation.

blocks.title: Using a Gauge Style

topics.title: VStack
topics.summary: A view that arranges its children in a vertical line.

blocks.textContent: VStack is one of the foundational layout containers in SwiftUI. It places child views from top to bottom, making it the default choice when content naturally reads as a vertical sequence.

blocks.title: Basic VStack

blocks.textContent: Spacing is a large part of what makes a stack feel intentional. You can rely on system spacing, but in tighter interfaces it is common to define a specific spacing value so the relationship between items stays consistent.

blocks.title: Controlling VStack Spacing

blocks.textContent: Alignment defines how children line up horizontally inside the vertical stack. Leading alignment is common for forms and reading-heavy layouts, while center alignment works better for compact, self-contained content blocks.

blocks.title: Leading Alignment in VStack

blocks.textContent: Most real SwiftUI screens are built by nesting stacks. VStack is rarely interesting on its own, but it becomes essential when combined with HStack, Spacer, padding, and frame modifiers to establish structure.

topics.title: HStack
topics.summary: A view that arranges its children in a horizontal line.

blocks.textContent: HStack lays out child views from leading to trailing. It is the standard container for rows, toolbars, inline metadata, and any composition where items should appear side by side.

blocks.title: Basic HStack

blocks.textContent: Horizontal spacing controls how dense or relaxed a row feels. Small spacing can make related content feel tightly grouped, while wider spacing helps separate actions or give individual elements more presence.

blocks.title: Controlling HStack Spacing

blocks.textContent: Alignment becomes more noticeable when the child views have different heights. You can align by center, top, bottom, first text baseline, or last text baseline depending on whether the row is more visual or text-driven.

blocks.title: Baseline Alignment in HStack

blocks.textContent: HStack is one of the most common building blocks for custom rows. Adding a Spacer between left and right content often gives you the canonical 'label on one side, value or action on the other' layout.

topics.title: ZStack
topics.summary: A view that overlays its children, aligning them in both axes.

blocks.textContent: ZStack places views on top of one another along the z-axis. It is useful when the layout depends on overlap, such as badges over icons, text on top of images, or loading states layered over existing content.

blocks.title: Basic ZStack

blocks.textContent: Like the other stacks, ZStack supports alignment. That matters when overlays should anchor to a corner or edge instead of sitting in the center, which is a common pattern for status indicators and decorative accents.

blocks.code:
ZStack(alignment: .topTrailing) {
    Image(systemName: "bell")
        .font(.largeTitle)

    Circle()
        .fill(.red)
        .frame(width: 12, height: 12)
}
blocks.title: Corner-Aligned Overlay

blocks.textContent: Order matters in a ZStack because later views appear above earlier ones. That simple rule gives you a lot of control, and zIndex is available when the visual stacking order needs to differ from the declaration order.
ZStack overlaps with overlay and background as a concept, but it is better suited when several peer views participate in the layered composition instead of one main view merely decorating another.

topics.title: Spacer
topics.summary: A flexible space that expands along the major axis of its containing stack layout.

blocks.textContent: Spacer is a flexible empty view that expands to take up available space inside a stack. It is one of the simplest but most important layout tools in SwiftUI because it lets you push content apart without hard-coding positions.

blocks.title: Basic Spacer

blocks.textContent: Spacer expands only along the major axis of the container it is in. In an HStack it grows horizontally, while in a VStack it grows vertically. That behavior is what makes it so predictable in stack-based layouts.

blocks.title: Spacer in a VStack

blocks.textContent: If needed, you can define a minimum length so the spacer does not collapse too aggressively. This is useful when the layout should stay flexible but still preserve a certain amount of breathing room.

blocks.title: Spacer with Minimum Length

topics.title: Divider
topics.summary: A visual element that can be used to separate other content.

blocks.textContent: Divider draws a thin separator line between pieces of content. It is a lightweight way to introduce visual separation without building a custom shape or managing explicit frames and colors yourself.

blocks.title: Basic Divider

blocks.textContent: Divider automatically adapts to the surrounding layout. In a VStack it usually appears as a horizontal line, while in an HStack it becomes vertical. That makes it feel natural inside stack-based compositions.

blocks.title: Divider in an HStack

blocks.textContent: Divider is intentionally subtle, which is often the right default. If a design calls for a stronger separator, it is usually better to style the surrounding layout carefully or replace Divider with a custom Rectangle rather than forcing it beyond its intended role.

topics.title: Group
topics.summary: A container for grouping view content.

blocks.textContent: Group is a lightweight container that lets you organize several child views without introducing a visible layout of its own. It is mostly about structure and composition rather than presentation.

blocks.title: Basic Group

blocks.textContent: The key idea is that Group does not behave like VStack or HStack. It does not add spacing, alignment, or layout rules on its own. Instead, it simply wraps several views so SwiftUI can treat them as one logical unit in certain contexts.

One practical use of Group is applying the same modifier to several sibling views at once. This keeps repeated styling or environment-related configuration in one place without creating an extra visible container.

blocks.code:
Group {
    Text("Home")
    Text("Explore")
    Text("Profile")
}
.font(.headline)
.foregroundStyle(.blue)
blocks.title: Applying Shared Modifiers

blocks.textContent: Group is also useful in conditional composition, especially when a branch needs to return multiple views. It helps keep the code readable while still respecting SwiftUI's view-building rules.

topics.title: ScrollView
topics.summary: A scrollable view.

blocks.textContent: ScrollView lets content extend beyond the bounds of the screen while remaining accessible through scrolling. It is one of the core containers for layouts that do not fit comfortably in a fixed space.

blocks.title: Basic ScrollView

blocks.textContent: By default, ScrollView scrolls vertically, but it can also scroll horizontally. Choosing the axis changes not only the movement direction but also how the rest of the layout should be composed around it.

blocks.title: Horizontal ScrollView

blocks.textContent: A ScrollView usually needs an internal layout container such as VStack, HStack, LazyVStack, or LazyHStack. Without that extra structure, arranging multiple child views predictably becomes awkward.

ScrollView gives you more layout freedom than List, but it also gives you less built-in behavior. If you need row semantics, editing, selection, or list-style conventions, List is often the better fit. If you need custom composition, ScrollView is usually the right tool.

topics.title: ScrollViewReader
topics.summary: A view that provides programmatic scrolling for its scrollable content.

blocks.textContent: ScrollViewReader wraps scrollable content and gives you access to a proxy that can scroll to specific child views by identifier. It is the standard way to move a ScrollView programmatically in SwiftUI.

blocks.title: Programmatic Scrolling

blocks.textContent: The critical detail is that the target views must have stable ids. The proxy can only scroll to something SwiftUI can identify, so using explicit ids is part of the design of the scrollable content.

Common use cases include chat timelines, validation that jumps to the first invalid field, deep-linking into long content, or restoring the user's position after an update. In all of these cases, the value comes from control over navigation within the scrollable area.

Programmatic scrolling is often paired with animation so the transition feels intentional rather than abrupt. That usually makes the movement easier to follow, especially when the jump is visually large.

blocks.title: Animated Scrolling

topics.title: LazyVStack
topics.summary: A container view that arranges its child views in a line that grows vertically, creating items only as needed.

blocks.textContent: LazyVStack is similar to VStack, but it creates its child views on demand as they become needed during scrolling. This makes it a better fit for larger vertical collections where eagerly building everything would be wasteful.

blocks.title: Basic LazyVStack

blocks.textContent: The practical difference from VStack appears when the content grows. For short, static layouts, VStack is simpler and perfectly fine. For long scrollable collections, LazyVStack usually scales better because it does less upfront work.

LazyVStack is almost always used inside a ScrollView. That pairing gives you custom scrolling layouts with better performance characteristics than a plain VStack, while still preserving more visual freedom than List.
It also supports pinned section headers, which is useful for grouped content that should keep context visible while the user scrolls. That makes it a strong tool for custom sectioned interfaces.

blocks.title: Pinned Section Headers

topics.title: LazyHStack
topics.summary: A container view that arranges its child views in a line that grows horizontally, creating items only as needed.

blocks.textContent: LazyHStack is the horizontal counterpart to LazyVStack. It lays out views from leading to trailing and creates them lazily, which is especially useful for horizontally scrolling carousels and galleries.

blocks.title: Basic LazyHStack

blocks.textContent: Its most common use case is a custom horizontal scroller where items are richer than a plain list row. Media carousels, onboarding cards, featured content strips, and storefront shelves are all natural fits.

As with LazyVStack, the main reason to choose the lazy variant is scale. If the horizontal content is small, HStack is often enough. If it can grow or become media-heavy, lazy creation is usually a better default.
Alignment and spacing still matter here, especially when cards have different heights. Being explicit about those values keeps horizontally scrolling content from feeling visually unstable.

topics.title: Grid
topics.summary: A container view that arranges other views in a two dimensional layout.

blocks.textContent: Grid lays out content in rows and columns using a true two-dimensional structure. It is useful when both horizontal and vertical relationships matter, and when the layout should read more like a table or dashboard than a simple stack.

blocks.title: Basic Grid

blocks.textContent: Unlike LazyVGrid and LazyHGrid, Grid is not primarily about scrolling collections. It is more about expressing an explicit matrix of views, which makes it well suited for small, structured layouts with clear row and column relationships.

Grid gives you alignment control across rows and columns, which is one of the reasons it can produce more coherent two-dimensional layouts than nesting stacks manually. When the layout needs to feel table-like, this matters a lot.

Good use cases include compact dashboards, comparison layouts, inspector-style panels, and any UI where aligning values across both axes improves readability. For long or data-heavy grids, the lazy grid variants are usually more appropriate.

topics.title: GridRow
topics.summary: A row of cells within a Grid.

blocks.textContent: GridRow represents a single horizontal row inside a Grid. It defines one set of cells that participate in the overall two-dimensional alignment of the grid.

blocks.title: Basic GridRow

blocks.textContent: GridRow is not useful on its own; its meaning comes from being inside Grid. Together they express the intent that rows and columns matter structurally, not just visually.

Rows participate in the grid's alignment behavior, which is what makes values line up consistently across columns. That is often cleaner than trying to recreate table-like alignment by nesting stacks.

topics.title: LazyVGrid
topics.summary: A container view that arranges its children in a grid that grows vertically, creating items only as needed.

blocks.textContent: LazyVGrid arranges items in columns while the content grows vertically, creating views only as needed during scrolling. It is one of the standard tools for gallery-like or card-based layouts in SwiftUI.

blocks.title: Basic LazyVGrid

blocks.textContent: The grid's behavior is driven by its column definitions. Fixed, flexible, and adaptive GridItem configurations let you describe whether the layout should stay rigid, stretch proportionally, or adapt to available width.

Common use cases include photo galleries, storefront grids, dashboards, and any scrollable collection where multiple columns improve scanning. It is often a better fit than List when the layout is more visual than row-oriented.
The distinction from Grid is mainly about scale and collection behavior. Grid is better for explicit small matrices; LazyVGrid is better for larger, scrollable data-driven content.

topics.title: LazyHGrid
topics.summary: A container view that arranges its children in a grid that grows horizontally, creating items only as needed.

blocks.textContent: LazyHGrid is the horizontal counterpart to LazyVGrid. It arranges items in rows while the content extends horizontally, making it useful for horizontally scrolling grid-like presentations.

blocks.title: Basic LazyHGrid

blocks.textContent: Instead of column definitions, LazyHGrid uses row definitions. That change reflects the core layout model: the content scrolls horizontally while the rows determine how items stack vertically.
It is most useful when you want the feel of a shelf or media browser but need more than one row visible at a time. This makes it a good fit for artwork, products, and other visually rich collections.

topics.title: AnyLayout
topics.summary: A type-erased layout value.

blocks.textContent: AnyLayout type-erases a concrete layout so you can switch between different layout strategies while keeping a stable view structure. This is especially useful when the UI needs to adapt between, for example, horizontal and vertical arrangements.

blocks.code:
let layout = isCompact
    ? AnyLayout(VStackLayout())
    : AnyLayout(HStackLayout())

layout {
    AvatarView()
    ProfileSummaryView()
}
blocks.title: Switching Layouts with AnyLayout

blocks.textContent: The main value is preserving identity while changing layout behavior. Without that, switching between different container types can cause the view hierarchy to be rebuilt in ways that feel less smooth or less predictable.

AnyLayout is particularly useful in adaptive interfaces that respond to size class, orientation, or content size. It gives you a more elegant alternative to large conditional branches that duplicate the same child views in different stacks.

topics.title: ViewThatFits
topics.summary: A view that adapts to the available space by providing the first child view that fits.

blocks.textContent: ViewThatFits evaluates several child views in order and displays the first one that fits the available space. It is a pragmatic tool for adaptive UI when you have a few known layout alternatives.

blocks.title: Choosing the First View That Fits

blocks.textContent: Order matters because SwiftUI stops at the first option that fits. That means you should place the most desirable layout first and then provide simpler fallbacks for tighter spaces.

Compared with AnyLayout, ViewThatFits is less about switching one layout algorithm and more about offering distinct view alternatives. It is a better fit when the fallbacks are meaningfully different compositions.

topics.title: GeometryReader
topics.summary: A container view that defines its content as a function of its own size and coordinate space.

blocks.textContent: GeometryReader exposes the size and coordinate information of its container so child views can react to the available space. It is one of SwiftUI's oldest layout tools for geometry-aware interfaces.

blocks.code:
GeometryReader { proxy in
    RoundedRectangle(cornerRadius: 16)
        .fill(.blue)
        .frame(width: proxy.size.width * 0.8)
}
blocks.title: Basic GeometryReader

blocks.textContent: GeometryReader is powerful, but it is easy to overuse. Because it expands to all available space by default, it can produce surprising layout results if you treat it like a passive measurement helper rather than an active container.
Common use cases include responsive sizing, scroll effects, parallax, reading positions in coordinate spaces, or building custom layout behaviors that depend on actual rendered geometry.

In modern SwiftUI, some problems that once required GeometryReader are now handled more cleanly with newer layout APIs. It is still useful, but it should usually be a deliberate choice rather than the default answer to every layout problem.

topics.title: GeometryProxy
topics.summary: A proxy for access to the size and coordinate space of the container view defined by a GeometryReader.

blocks.textContent: GeometryProxy is the value you receive inside GeometryReader. It provides access to measurements such as size, safe area insets, and frame information in different coordinate spaces.
The most common property is size, which tells you how much room the GeometryReader currently occupies. That is often enough for proportional sizing and adaptive decisions.

blocks.title: Reading a Frame from GeometryProxy

blocks.textContent: Its frame-reading APIs become more interesting when coordinate spaces are involved. This is where GeometryProxy moves beyond raw size and starts enabling scroll tracking, visibility effects, and position-aware interactions.

topics.title: coordinateSpace
topics.summary: Assigns a name to the view's local coordinate space.

blocks.textContent: coordinateSpace names a view's coordinate system so geometry can be read relative to it later. It is mostly used together with GeometryReader and GeometryProxy when global or local coordinates are not enough.

blocks.title: Naming a Coordinate Space

blocks.textContent: Named coordinate spaces are useful when you need to measure a child relative to a specific container rather than the whole screen. This is common in custom scrolling effects and sticky interface elements.

blocks.title: Reading in a Named Coordinate Space

topics.title: Layout
topics.summary: A type that computes positions for views within a container.

blocks.textContent: Layout is the protocol behind SwiftUI's modern custom layout system. It lets you define how child views are measured and positioned, which makes it the foundation for reusable layout behavior beyond the built-in stacks and grids.

A custom layout typically implements methods for sizing and placement. Those two responsibilities mirror the core of layout work: decide how much space is needed, then decide where each subview goes inside it.

blocks.title: Basic Layout Conformance

blocks.textContent: The big advantage is reusability. Instead of scattering geometry logic across multiple screens, you can encode layout behavior as a first-class type and apply it declaratively wherever needed.

topics.title: Custom Layouts
topics.summary: Reusable layout types built with SwiftUI's Layout protocol.

blocks.textContent: Custom layouts are user-defined layout containers built on top of the Layout protocol. They let you move from one-off view composition into reusable layout primitives tailored to your app's design language.

They become valuable when the built-in stacks and grids are close but not quite enough. If the same nonstandard arrangement appears in several places, a custom layout is often cleaner than repeating geometry math or deeply nesting containers.

Examples include tag clouds, equal-width button rows, masonry-inspired flows, radial menus, and adaptive dashboard arrangements. The common thread is that layout itself becomes a reusable abstraction instead of an incidental detail.

blocks.title: Using a Custom Layout

blocks.textContent: Custom layouts are powerful, but they also raise the abstraction level of the codebase. They are worth it when the behavior is reused or meaningfully expressive, not when a simple stack and a few modifiers would already solve the problem cleanly.

topics.title: List
topics.summary: A container that presents rows of data arranged in a single column.

blocks.textContent: List is SwiftUI's standard container for vertically scrolling rows. It combines layout, scrolling, platform styling, and row behavior into a single component, which makes it one of the most practical views in the framework.

blocks.title: Basic List

blocks.textContent: List becomes most useful when it is driven by data. In practice, it is often paired with ForEach and identifiable models so rows can be created, updated, and removed in a way that stays consistent with SwiftUI's diffing system.

blocks.title: Data-Driven List

blocks.textContent: Compared with ScrollView, List gives you more built-in behavior and stronger platform conventions. That is usually a benefit when the content is naturally row-based, but it can feel restrictive if the screen needs highly custom layout composition.

Lists also work especially well with Section, selection APIs, swipe actions, editing, and listStyle. Because of that, they are often the backbone of settings screens, inboxes, menus, and structured collections of app data.

topics.title: ForEach
topics.summary: A structure that computes views on demand from an underlying collection of identified data.

blocks.textContent: ForEach generates repeated views from a collection of data. It is one of the main bridges between your model layer and your interface, because it turns arrays and ranges into structured visual output.

blocks.title: Basic ForEach

blocks.textContent: Identity is the key concept. SwiftUI needs to know which element is which across updates, so ForEach works best with Identifiable data or explicit ids. Without stable identity, animations, updates, and state preservation can behave unexpectedly.

blocks.title: ForEach with Identifiable Data

blocks.textContent: ForEach is often seen inside List, stacks, grids, and ScrollView. It is not a scrolling container or layout engine by itself; instead, it participates in whichever parent layout it is placed inside.

topics.title: Section
topics.summary: A container view that groups related content and can provide optional header and footer views.

blocks.textContent: Section groups related content inside containers such as List and Form. It gives structure to longer interfaces by separating content into smaller, named parts that are easier to scan.

blocks.title: Basic Section

blocks.textContent: Headers and footers are not just decoration. They often carry meaning, context, or guidance, especially in forms and settings screens where the user benefits from understanding why a group of controls exists.

blocks.title: Section with a Footer

blocks.textContent: Section is most effective when the content really belongs together conceptually. If every row becomes its own section, the interface usually starts to feel over-separated and visually noisy.

topics.title: Table
topics.summary: A container that presents data in a two-dimensional table format.

blocks.textContent: Table presents structured data in rows and columns. It is designed for interfaces where comparing values across fields matters more than the looser, single-column rhythm of a List.

blocks.title: Basic Table

blocks.textContent: Table is best when the user needs to scan and compare data horizontally and vertically. That makes it particularly suitable for desktop-style experiences, management interfaces, and information-dense tools.

Even though Table exists in SwiftUI, it is more at home in platforms and contexts where multi-column data presentation feels natural. For many iPhone-first experiences, List remains the more idiomatic choice.

topics.title: Form
topics.summary: A container for grouping controls used for data entry, such as in settings or inspectors.

blocks.textContent: Form is a container designed for grouped data entry and settings-style interfaces. It provides a structured surface for controls like TextField, Toggle, Picker, Stepper, and DatePicker while adopting platform-appropriate styling automatically.

blocks.title: Basic Form

blocks.textContent: Form becomes much more effective when paired with Section. Grouping related controls into labeled areas makes long forms easier to understand and helps the interface feel deliberate instead of like a random sequence of inputs.

blocks.title: Form with Sections

blocks.textContent: Form and List can look related, but their intent is different. List is generally about browsing or selecting data, while Form is about editing, configuring, or entering values. Choosing the right one makes the screen's purpose clearer immediately.

Good use cases include settings screens, profile editors, account setup, and inspection-style UIs. If the interface is primarily about manipulating values rather than reading a collection, Form is usually the more semantically correct container.

topics.title: NavigationStack
topics.summary: A view that presents a root view and enables navigation to additional views.

blocks.textContent: NavigationStack is SwiftUI's modern container for push-style navigation. It manages a stack of destinations and replaces the older NavigationView approach for most new apps.

blocks.title: Basic NavigationStack

blocks.textContent: A major strength of NavigationStack is that it supports data-driven navigation. Instead of wiring destinations only through view hierarchy, you can describe navigation state with values and paths, which makes deeper flows easier to control.
In modern SwiftUI, NavigationStack is usually the right root container for hierarchical navigation on iPhone. It works especially well with NavigationLink, NavigationPath, and navigationDestination.

topics.title: NavigationLink
topics.summary: A control that presents a destination view when triggered.

blocks.textContent: NavigationLink is the standard control for moving from one screen to another inside a navigation container. It often appears as a row in a list, but it can also be styled and used more freely in custom layouts.

blocks.title: Basic NavigationLink

blocks.textContent: In newer navigation APIs, NavigationLink often works with values rather than direct destination closures. That lets SwiftUI resolve the destination through navigationDestination, which scales better in more complex flows.

blocks.code: NavigationLink("Open Settings", value: Route.settings)
blocks.title: Value-Based NavigationLink

blocks.textContent: Using NavigationLink makes navigation intent explicit in the view hierarchy. That is usually preferable to building button-based push behavior manually, because it stays more aligned with SwiftUI's navigation model.

topics.title: NavigationPath
topics.summary: A type-erased list of data representing the content of a navigation stack.

blocks.textContent: NavigationPath stores the current contents of a NavigationStack in a type-erased form. It allows programmatic navigation that is driven by data rather than only by taps in the view tree.

blocks.title: Programmatic Navigation with NavigationPath

blocks.textContent: This is useful for deep linking, restoring navigation state, coordinating multi-step flows, and jumping to destinations based on app events. It gives you far more control than purely view-triggered navigation.

NavigationPath is powerful, but it also raises the complexity of navigation state. It is best used when the app genuinely benefits from programmatic path control rather than for simple one-step pushes.

topics.title: TabView
topics.summary: A view that switches between multiple child views using interactive user interface elements.

blocks.textContent: TabView organizes an app into peer sections that users can switch between. On iPhone, it commonly maps to the bottom tab bar, making it one of the primary structural containers for app-level navigation.

blocks.title: Basic TabView

blocks.textContent: TabView can also be driven by a selection binding, which is useful when the app needs to switch tabs programmatically or keep tab state in sync with a larger navigation model.

blocks.title: TabView with Selection

topics.title: sheet
topics.summary: Presents a sheet when a binding to a Boolean value or an item is true.

blocks.textContent: sheet presents content modally over the current interface. It is typically used for secondary flows that should feel separate from the current screen but not replace the entire app experience.

blocks.title: Basic Sheet Presentation

blocks.textContent: Sheets can be driven by a simple Boolean or by an optional item. The item-based version is often cleaner when the presentation depends on selected data instead of a generic visible-or-hidden state.

A sheet usually implies a self-contained task like editing, composing, picking, or onboarding. If the user should move deeper into app hierarchy rather than step into a modal task, navigation may be a better fit than a sheet.

topics.title: fullScreenCover
topics.summary: Presents a modal view that covers as much of the screen as possible.

blocks.textContent: fullScreenCover presents modal content that covers the full screen. It is appropriate when the presented experience should feel immersive or when the distinction from the previous screen should be especially strong.

blocks.title: Basic Full-Screen Cover

blocks.textContent: Compared with sheet, fullScreenCover communicates a stronger transition. It is commonly used for onboarding, authentication gates, media experiences, or flows that should temporarily take over the interface.

topics.title: popover
topics.summary: Presents a popover when a given condition is true.

blocks.textContent: popover presents temporary content anchored to another view. It is useful for contextual information, lightweight editing, or additional controls that should appear close to the triggering element.

blocks.title: Basic Popover

blocks.textContent: Popover behavior is more central on iPad and macOS than on iPhone. Even so, it is still useful to understand conceptually because it expresses a specific UX intent: contextual presentation rather than full navigation or broad modal takeover.

topics.title: alert
topics.summary: Presents an alert to the user.

blocks.textContent: alert presents a focused modal message that asks the user to acknowledge something or choose between a small number of actions. It is best reserved for important information, errors, and decisions that deserve immediate attention.

blocks.title: Basic Alert

blocks.textContent: Alerts are intentionally interruptive, so they work best when used sparingly. If the message is not critical or the action is not high-stakes, a less disruptive surface is often better.

topics.title: confirmationDialog
topics.summary: Presents a dialog that asks the user to confirm an action.

blocks.textContent: confirmationDialog presents a set of actions for the user to choose from, usually when they need to confirm intent or pick one of several related options. It is less severe than an alert and often better suited to action selection.

blocks.title: Basic Confirmation Dialog

blocks.textContent: Compared with alert, confirmationDialog is usually better when the user is choosing among actions rather than responding to a warning or blocking message. It feels more like an action sheet than an interruption.

topics.title: navigationTitle
topics.summary: Sets the title of the navigation bar for this view.

blocks.textContent: navigationTitle sets the title shown by the surrounding navigation container. It is one of the simplest but most visible navigation modifiers because it directly shapes how a screen is identified to the user.

blocks.title: Basic navigationTitle

blocks.textContent: The modifier belongs on the destination content rather than on the stack container itself in most cases. That keeps each screen responsible for its own title and usually leads to cleaner composition.
Good navigation titles are usually short and descriptive. Since they carry a lot of orientation value, vague titles can make the hierarchy feel less clear even when the rest of the layout is correct.

topics.title: navigationBarTitleDisplayMode
topics.summary: Configures the title display mode for this view.

blocks.textContent: navigationBarTitleDisplayMode controls whether a navigation title appears in large, inline, or automatic style. It affects the tone and visual hierarchy of a screen more than it may seem at first glance.

blocks.title: Inline Title Display Mode

blocks.textContent: Large titles often work well for top-level destinations because they create stronger hierarchy and breathing room. Inline titles usually fit better for deeper screens or denser interfaces where conserving vertical space matters more.
Automatic lets the system choose based on context, which is often fine. But being explicit is helpful when a screen's visual role in the hierarchy is intentional and should not vary implicitly.

topics.title: toolbar
topics.summary: Populates the toolbar or navigation bar with the specified items.

blocks.textContent: toolbar is the main modifier for adding actions and controls to navigation bars, bottom bars, and other toolbar placements. It centralizes command-like UI so screens can expose actions without embedding them directly in the content layout.

blocks.title: Basic toolbar Modifier

blocks.textContent: Placement matters because it defines both location and semantic importance. Primary actions, cancellation, confirmation, editing, and bottom bar actions each communicate something slightly different through placement.
The toolbar modifier is most valuable when actions belong to the screen as a whole rather than to one local component. It helps keep the content area focused while still exposing important commands.

topics.title: ToolbarItem
topics.summary: A model that represents a single item in a toolbar.

blocks.textContent: ToolbarItem defines a single piece of toolbar content and its placement. It is the building block used inside the toolbar modifier whenever one action or control should appear in a specific position.

blocks.title: Basic ToolbarItem

blocks.textContent: Its placement determines how the system interprets the item. That means ToolbarItem is not just about position on screen; it also participates in the navigation and toolbar conventions of the platform.
Use ToolbarItem when the content is singular and has a clear placement. If several related items belong together, ToolbarItemGroup usually expresses that intent more cleanly.

topics.title: ToolbarItemGroup
topics.summary: A model that groups multiple toolbar items together at a specified placement.

blocks.textContent: ToolbarItemGroup lets you place several related toolbar items together in the same area. It is useful when a screen has a small cluster of actions that belong in one placement rather than scattered across the bar.

blocks.title: Basic ToolbarItemGroup

blocks.textContent: Grouping items helps the relationship between actions stay visible in code and in the resulting UI. It is especially useful when the actions are complementary, sequential, or belong to the same mode of interaction.
Even with ToolbarItemGroup, restraint matters. Toolbars become harder to scan when too many actions compete for the same space, so grouping should clarify intent rather than just increase density.

topics.title: @State
topics.summary: A property wrapper type that can read and write a value managed by SwiftUI.

blocks.textContent: @State is the basic tool for local mutable state owned by a view. When the value changes, SwiftUI recomputes the body and updates the interface to reflect the new state.

blocks.title: Basic @State

blocks.textContent: The key idea is ownership. If the view should own and mutate a simple piece of value state itself, @State is usually the right tool. If the value is owned elsewhere, another wrapper is often more appropriate.

@State is best suited to local value-oriented state such as toggles, counters, text input, selection, and lightweight view modes. It is not usually the right place for shared app models or long-lived reference types.

topics.title: @Binding
topics.summary: A property wrapper type that can read and write a value owned by a source of truth elsewhere.

blocks.textContent: @Binding creates a two-way connection to state that is owned somewhere else. It lets child views read and write a value without taking ownership of it.

blocks.title: Basic @Binding

blocks.textContent: Bindings are one of the main ways parent and child views collaborate in SwiftUI. The parent owns the source of truth, while the child receives controlled write access to that value.

blocks.title: Passing a Binding from @State

blocks.textContent: A useful rule is simple: use @State where data is owned, and use @Binding where data is borrowed. That distinction makes data flow much easier to reason about in real projects.

topics.title: @StateObject
topics.summary: A property wrapper type that instantiates and owns an observable object.

blocks.textContent: @StateObject is the legacy Combine-era wrapper for creating and owning an ObservableObject inside a SwiftUI view. It keeps the reference alive across view updates and prevents accidental reinitialization.

blocks.title: Owning an ObservableObject with @StateObject

blocks.textContent: Use @StateObject when the view should create and own the lifetime of a reference-type model in the older ObservableObject system. If the object comes from outside, @ObservedObject is usually the better fit.

In codebases that still use Combine-based observation, @StateObject remains important. In newer Observation-based code, some of this role shifts toward plain stored models and different bindings patterns, but @StateObject still matters for backward-compatible architectures.

topics.title: @ObservedObject
topics.summary: A property wrapper type that subscribes to an observable object and invalidates a view whenever the observable object changes.

blocks.textContent: @ObservedObject connects a view to an ObservableObject that is owned elsewhere. The view observes changes, but it does not control the model's lifetime.

blocks.title: Basic @ObservedObject

blocks.textContent: The crucial difference from @StateObject is ownership. If the view creates the object, use @StateObject. If the view receives the object, use @ObservedObject.

@ObservedObject belongs to the older Combine-oriented observation model, but it is still common in many production SwiftUI codebases. Understanding it is important even when adopting newer Observation APIs gradually.

topics.title: @EnvironmentObject
topics.summary: A property wrapper type for an observable object supplied by a parent or ancestor view.

blocks.textContent: @EnvironmentObject injects an ObservableObject through the view hierarchy so descendants can access it without every intermediate view passing it manually.

blocks.title: Basic @EnvironmentObject

blocks.textContent: This is useful for shared app-wide or feature-wide state such as session data, theme preferences, or navigation coordination. It reduces prop drilling, but it also makes dependencies less explicit, so it should be used with care.

blocks.code:
RootView()
    .environmentObject(SessionStore())
blocks.title: Injecting an Environment Object

blocks.textContent: Because the dependency is implicit, missing environment injection often causes runtime issues. That is one reason some teams prefer more explicit dependency flow when the shared state is not truly global.

topics.title: ObservableObject
topics.summary: An object with a publisher that emits before the object has changed.

blocks.textContent: ObservableObject is the Combine-era protocol for reference models that can notify SwiftUI when something is about to change. It is the foundation behind @StateObject, @ObservedObject, and @EnvironmentObject.

blocks.title: Basic ObservableObject

blocks.textContent: This protocol made shared reference models feel natural in early SwiftUI, especially alongside Combine. It still matters in many apps, but it now sits beside newer Observation APIs rather than being the only recommended path.

Conceptually, ObservableObject solves the same broad problem as @Observable in the newer system: it lets UI react to model changes. The difference is the observation mechanism and the surrounding API surface.

topics.title: @Published
topics.summary: A type that publishes a property marked with an attribute.

blocks.textContent: @Published marks properties inside an ObservableObject so changes trigger object updates that SwiftUI and Combine subscribers can react to.

blocks.title: Basic @Published

blocks.textContent: Without @Published, a property on an ObservableObject will not automatically trigger view updates just by changing. That is why it became such a central part of the older SwiftUI observation model.

In modern Observation-based code, @Published is often no longer necessary for model change tracking. It remains important, however, in Combine-oriented architectures and for codebases that still rely on ObservableObject.

topics.title: @Observable
topics.summary: A macro that adds observation support to a type.

blocks.textContent: @Observable is part of the Observation framework and represents the newer approach to model observation in SwiftUI. It reduces some of the ceremony of ObservableObject and @Published while fitting better with Swift's newer language features.

blocks.title: Basic @Observable

blocks.textContent: Compared with ObservableObject, the newer Observation model often feels more direct. You write regular properties, and the macro provides the observation behavior, which makes the model read more like plain Swift.

This is the modern direction for SwiftUI state models, but many projects still straddle both systems. In practice, teams often need to understand both the older Combine-based model and the newer Observation framework at the same time.

topics.title: @Bindable
topics.summary: A property wrapper that supports creating bindings to properties of an observable object.

blocks.textContent: @Bindable works with the Observation framework and makes it easy to create bindings into an @Observable model. It fills a role that often required more ceremony in older data flow approaches.

blocks.title: Basic @Bindable

blocks.textContent: Its main purpose is to let controls like TextField, Toggle, and Picker bind directly into observable model properties without forcing those properties to live in a separate local @State value first.

@Bindable makes the newer observation model feel much more natural in forms and editable UIs. It is one of the reasons the Observation framework can replace some older wrapper combinations in modern SwiftUI code.

topics.title: @AppStorage
topics.summary: A property wrapper that reads and writes a value from UserDefaults.

blocks.textContent: @AppStorage connects SwiftUI state to UserDefaults so a value can persist across app launches. It is convenient for lightweight preferences and app-level settings.

blocks.code:
@AppStorage("isDarkModeEnabled") private var isDarkModeEnabled = false

Toggle("Dark Mode", isOn: $isDarkModeEnabled)
blocks.title: Basic @AppStorage

blocks.textContent: This is a good fit for small persisted values like feature flags, onboarding completion, selected theme, or user preferences. It is not a substitute for richer persistence models or databases.
@AppStorage is interesting because it sits at the intersection of state and persistence. The value behaves like view state in code, but it is backed by storage rather than existing only in memory.

topics.title: @SceneStorage
topics.summary: A property wrapper that reads and writes a value associated with the current scene.

blocks.textContent: @SceneStorage preserves small pieces of UI state for a specific scene, which makes it useful for restoring in-progress interface context such as selection, draft text, or tab position.

blocks.title: Basic @SceneStorage

blocks.textContent: The main difference from @AppStorage is scope. AppStorage is app-wide and user-defaults based, while SceneStorage is tied to a particular scene and is more about restoring transient interface context than long-term preference storage.
Use it for lightweight restoration rather than as a general state model. It works best when the value helps the interface resume smoothly, not when the data represents core business state.

topics.title: @Environment
topics.summary: A property wrapper that reads a value from a view's environment.

blocks.textContent: @Environment reads values that SwiftUI makes available through the view hierarchy. These values describe context such as presentation behavior, appearance, lifecycle state, and system capabilities.

blocks.title: Basic @Environment

blocks.textContent: The environment is useful when a view needs contextual information but should not have that information passed manually through every intermediate layer. It gives SwiftUI a clean way to expose shared context without turning every dependency into an explicit parameter.
@Environment is usually for reading individual values, while @EnvironmentObject is for shared observable models. They are related ideas, but they solve different kinds of dependency flow.

topics.title: colorScheme
topics.summary: An environment value that indicates whether the interface uses a light or dark appearance.

blocks.textContent: colorScheme tells a view whether the current environment is using light or dark appearance. It is one of the most commonly read environment values because it helps custom UI adapt visually to system appearance.

blocks.title: Reading colorScheme

blocks.textContent: Most of the time, using semantic colors and materials is better than branching manually on colorScheme. Reading the value is most useful when a custom drawing or custom visual decision genuinely depends on the current appearance.

topics.title: dismiss
topics.summary: An action that dismisses the current presentation.

blocks.textContent: dismiss is an environment-provided action that tells the current presentation or navigation context to close itself. It is the modern SwiftUI way to dismiss sheets, popovers, and pushed destinations from inside the presented view.

blocks.title: Using dismiss

blocks.textContent: The meaning of dismiss depends on context. In a sheet it dismisses the modal; in a navigation stack it pops the current destination; in other contexts it may have no effect. That is why it feels simple in code but still context-sensitive in behavior.

topics.title: openURL
topics.summary: An action that opens a URL.
