blocks.textContent: NotificationCenter.Publisher turns classic NotificationCenter events into a Combine stream. It is a practical bridge for integrating older event-driven APIs into reactive code.

blocks.code:
let cancellable = NotificationCenter.default
    .publisher(for: UIApplication.didBecomeActiveNotification)
    .sink { _ in
        print("App became active")
    }
blocks.title: Observing Notifications

blocks.textContent: This is especially useful when modernizing existing code incrementally rather than rewriting every event source from scratch.

topics.title: Timer.TimerPublisher
topics.summary: Publishes values on a repeating timer schedule.

blocks.textContent: Timer.TimerPublisher is Combine's built-in way to model repeated time-based events. It publishes dates on a schedule, making timers fit naturally into pipelines.

blocks.code:
let publisher = Timer.publish(every: 1.0, on: .main, in: .common)
    .autoconnect()
blocks.title: Publishing Timer Ticks

blocks.textContent: This is useful for countdowns, periodic refreshes, and UI that depends on ongoing time-based updates.

topics.title: Demand
topics.summary: Represents how many values a subscriber is ready to receive.

blocks.textContent: Demand is Combine's backpressure mechanism. It expresses how many values a subscriber is prepared to receive, allowing the consumer side to influence the pace of the stream.
Most everyday Combine code hides this detail behind sink and assign, but it becomes important when building custom subscribers or reasoning about performance-sensitive streams.

blocks.code:
let unlimited = Subscribers.Demand.unlimited
let one = Subscribers.Demand.max(1)
blocks.title: Common Demand Values

topics.title: request(_:)
topics.summary: Requests additional demand from a subscription.

blocks.textContent: request(_:) is how a subscriber tells a subscription how much more demand it wants. This is one of the low-level control points that makes Combine more disciplined than fire-and-forget event systems.

blocks.title: Requesting More Values

blocks.textContent: You rarely write this in app-level code, but understanding it makes subscriber behavior and backpressure far less magical.

topics.title: values
topics.summary: Exposes a publisher's output as an async sequence.

blocks.textContent: values is one of the clearest bridges between Combine and Swift Concurrency. It exposes publisher output as an async sequence, allowing reactive streams to be consumed with for await syntax.

blocks.title: Consuming Publisher Values Asynchronously

blocks.textContent: This is valuable when newer async code needs to interact with an existing Combine-based layer without rewriting everything at once.

topics.title: AsyncSequence Bridge
topics.summary: Describes the conceptual bridge between Combine publishers and AsyncSequence consumers.

blocks.textContent: The AsyncSequence bridge is the conceptual meeting point between Combine and structured concurrency. It lets stream-based data move into an async-await style without losing the idea of values arriving over time.
This matters because Combine models push-based streams, while async-await often feels pull-based and sequential. Bridging gives you a practical way to mix both worlds intentionally.

blocks.title: Conceptual Bridge in Practice

topics.title: async/await Integration
topics.summary: Uses Combine and Swift Concurrency together in the same architecture.

blocks.textContent: async/await integration is less about one specific API and more about architectural coexistence. Many codebases now mix Combine pipelines with Task-based flows, and the important skill is choosing the right boundary between them.
A common pattern is to keep existing publishers for observation and UI binding, while newer async functions handle request-response style operations more directly.

blocks.title: Mixing Both Models

topics.title: Constants and Variables
topics.summary: Stores named values using let for constants and var for mutable state.

blocks.textContent: Constants and variables are the starting point for almost every Swift declaration. Both give a name to a value, but Swift draws a strong line between something that stays fixed and something that is expected to change over time.

blocks.code:
let maximumRetries = 3
var currentAttempt = 0

currentAttempt += 1
blocks.title: Declaring a Constant and a Variable

blocks.textContent: Use let by default. That small habit makes intent clearer, prevents accidental mutation, and usually leads to code that is easier to reason about. Reach for var only when the value genuinely needs to evolve.

Swift also infers the type from the initial value, so declarations often stay compact. In practice, that means many values can be introduced with almost no ceremony while still remaining strongly typed.

blocks.code:
let firstName = "Thiago", lastName = "Castro"
var x = 0.0, y = 0.0, z = 0.0
blocks.title: Multiple Declarations

blocks.textContent: When the assigned value already makes the type obvious, inference is usually enough. When the type carries architectural meaning or the initializer is ambiguous, an explicit annotation makes the declaration easier to read.

topics.title: Type Annotations
topics.summary: Declares the intended type explicitly when inference alone is not enough.

blocks.textContent: Swift prefers type inference, but explicit type annotations are still an important part of the language. They help when you want to communicate intent, constrain an API boundary, or make sure a value starts with the exact type you expect.

blocks.code:
let languageName: String = "Swift"
var progress: Double = 0.0
blocks.title: Basic Type Annotations

blocks.textContent: The syntax is simple: write a colon after the name, then the type. That pattern appears everywhere in Swift, including properties, parameters, tuple members, closures, and generic constraints, so getting comfortable with it pays off quickly.

Annotations become especially useful when a literal could fit more than one type. A plain 0 could be an Int, Double, CGFloat, or something else entirely depending on context. Being explicit avoids hidden assumptions.

blocks.code:
let tags: [String] = ["ios", "swift", "swiftui"]
let emptyScores: [Int] = []
blocks.title: Annotating Collection Types

blocks.textContent: Good Swift code balances inference and explicitness. If every line is annotated, the code gets noisy. If nothing is annotated, important intent disappears. The right choice is usually the one that makes the declaration obvious on first read.

topics.title: Strings and Characters
topics.summary: Represents textual data using String and individual extended grapheme clusters with Character.

blocks.textContent: Strings in Swift are Unicode-correct and designed for real-world text, not just ASCII. That matters because user-facing text can contain emoji, accents, composed symbols, and scripts from many languages without falling into the common traps found in simpler string models.

blocks.code:
let title = "Swift Atlas"
let initial: Character = "S"
blocks.title: String and Character

blocks.textContent: Most everyday string building uses interpolation. It is clearer than manual concatenation, easier to scan, and works naturally when you need to embed values into user-facing messages.

blocks.code:
let framework = "SwiftUI"
let message = "Learning \(framework) with Swift"
blocks.title: String Interpolation

blocks.textContent: Swift also supports multiline string literals, which are useful for formatted text, embedded examples, and content that should stay readable in source code. They reduce escaping noise and make larger text blocks easier to maintain.

blocks.code:
let quote = """
Swift is expressive,
strongly typed,
and built for clarity.
"""
blocks.title: Multiline String

blocks.textContent: Because Swift strings are Unicode-aware, operations like counting characters are based on human-readable characters rather than raw bytes. That is usually what you want in app development, but it is worth remembering when performance or indexing behavior matters.

topics.title: Numbers and Booleans
topics.summary: Works with integer, floating-point, and Boolean values using explicit, type-safe rules.

blocks.textContent: Swift treats numeric types and booleans with deliberate strictness. Integers, floating-point values, and Bool each have clear roles, and the language avoids many of the silent conversions that make code convenient in the short term but error-prone over time.

blocks.code:
let itemCount = 42
let averageRating = 4.8
let isEnabled = true
blocks.title: Basic Numeric and Boolean Values

blocks.textContent: Type inference handles most literals well, but Swift still keeps the resulting type precise. For example, an integer literal becomes an Int by default, while decimal literals become Double unless context suggests something else.

Swift does not automatically mix numeric types in expressions. If an Int needs to interact with a Double, you convert intentionally. That extra step may seem strict at first, but it prevents subtle bugs and makes arithmetic rules easier to trust.

blocks.code:
let steps = 3
let pace = 1.5
let total = Double(steps) * pace
blocks.title: Explicit Numeric Conversion

blocks.textContent: Bool is equally strict. In Swift, conditions must evaluate to true or false directly. You cannot use 0, 1, or arbitrary objects as truthy stand-ins, which keeps conditional logic much clearer.

topics.title: Tuples
topics.summary: Groups multiple values into a single compound value without creating a custom type.

blocks.textContent: Tuples let you bundle several related values into a single compound value. They are ideal for lightweight grouping when creating a full struct or class would be unnecessary overhead.

blocks.title: Named Tuple Elements

blocks.textContent: Tuple members can be accessed by position or by name when labels are provided. Named elements usually read better, especially when the tuple leaves the local scope or represents a meaningful pair of values.

blocks.code:
let person = ("Thiago", 31)
let (name, age) = person

print(name)
print(age)
blocks.title: Tuple Decomposition

blocks.textContent: Tuples are best for temporary structure. If the same shape appears throughout the codebase, or if the values need behavior and semantics, that is usually the point where a dedicated type becomes the better model.

topics.title: Comments
topics.summary: Uses single-line and multiline comments to explain intent without affecting execution.

blocks.textContent: Comments are ignored by the compiler, but they still shape how maintainable a codebase feels. In Swift, the best comments usually explain why something exists, not what a straightforward line of code is already saying.

blocks.code:
// Retry once if the token is expired
refreshSessionIfNeeded()
blocks.title: Single-Line Comment

blocks.textContent: Swift supports both single-line and multiline comments, and multiline comments can even nest. That last detail is surprisingly useful when temporarily commenting out a larger block that already contains comments inside it.

blocks.code:
/*
 This parser is intentionally strict because
 the backend payload is versioned separately.
*/
parseResponse(data)
blocks.title: Multiline Comment

blocks.textContent: If a function needs many comments to become understandable, the better fix is often to rename things or split the logic. In other words, comments are valuable, but they should support clarity, not compensate for missing structure.

topics.title: Arithmetic Operators
topics.summary: Performs numeric work like addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, and remainder.

blocks.textContent: Arithmetic operators are the basic tools for numeric computation in Swift. They behave predictably, respect the operand types involved, and avoid the kind of hidden coercions that often make arithmetic harder to trust in looser languages.

blocks.code:
let sum = 10 + 5
let difference = 10 - 5
let product = 10 * 5
let quotient = 10 / 5
let remainder = 10 % 3
blocks.title: Basic Arithmetic

blocks.textContent: The important detail is that arithmetic in Swift stays type-safe. Integer division produces an integer result, and mixed numeric expressions usually require explicit conversion, which makes the outcome much easier to reason about.

topics.title: Comparison Operators
topics.summary: Compares values using operators like ==, !=, <, >, <=, and >=.

blocks.textContent: Comparison operators answer yes-or-no questions about values. They are the backbone of conditions, filtering rules, validation, and most decision-making code in Swift.

blocks.code:
let isEqual = 3 == 3
let isDifferent = 3 != 4
let isGreater = 10 > 2
let isInOrder = 2 <= 5
blocks.title: Common Comparisons

blocks.textContent: Because they produce Bool, comparison operators connect naturally to if, guard, while, and switch conditions. Good Swift code tends to keep these expressions direct and readable rather than overly compact.

topics.title: Logical Operators
topics.summary: Combines or inverts Boolean expressions with !, &&, and ||.

blocks.textContent: Logical operators work with Bool values to negate conditions, require multiple truths, or allow multiple valid paths. They are small operators, but they shape a large share of everyday control flow.

blocks.code:
let isLoggedIn = true
let hasSubscription = false

let canAccessPremium = isLoggedIn && hasSubscription
let needsPaywall = !hasSubscription
let canEnter = isLoggedIn || hasSubscription
blocks.title: Negation, AND, and OR

blocks.textContent: Swift also short-circuits logical expressions. That means && stops as soon as the result is already false, and || stops as soon as the result is already true. This matters for both performance and safety when later checks depend on earlier ones.

topics.title: Ternary Operator
topics.summary: Chooses between two expressions using a compact condition ? trueValue : falseValue.

blocks.textContent: The ternary conditional operator is Swift's compact way to choose one of two expressions. It is best when the decision is small, obvious, and easier to read inline than as a full if statement.

blocks.code:
let isPro = true
let badgeText = isPro ? "PRO" : "FREE"
blocks.title: Basic Ternary Expression

blocks.textContent: The tradeoff is readability. A single, simple choice is a good fit. Once either branch becomes long or nested, a regular if usually communicates intent much better.

topics.title: Range Operators
topics.summary: Creates closed, half-open, and one-sided ranges for iteration and slicing.

blocks.textContent: Range operators describe intervals of values. In Swift they are especially useful for loops, collection slicing, and APIs that need a clear notion of bounds without introducing custom logic.

blocks.title: Closed and Half-Open Ranges

blocks.textContent: Swift also supports one-sided ranges like ..<5 or 3..., which are especially useful when slicing collections. The key is to choose the form that matches the boundary rules you actually mean.

topics.title: Custom Operators
topics.summary: Defines new operators with custom precedence and behavior when domain-specific syntax is justified.

blocks.textContent: Swift allows you to define custom operators, but this is a power feature, not an everyday habit. It can make domain-specific code elegant, yet it can also make ordinary code obscure if overused.

blocks.title: Defining a Custom Operator

blocks.textContent: The real question is not whether Swift can support a custom operator, but whether the team can read it six months later. In most app code, named functions age better unless the operator maps to a truly clear domain concept.

topics.title: if
topics.summary: Runs code conditionally when a Boolean expression evaluates to true.

blocks.textContent: if is the most direct way to express conditional execution in Swift. It keeps simple decisions readable and works best when the condition tells a clear story about why a branch should run.

blocks.title: Basic if Statement

blocks.textContent: Swift requires the condition to be Bool. That strictness is a feature, not a limitation, because it keeps intent explicit and avoids truthy or falsy surprises.

topics.title: switch
topics.summary: Matches a value against multiple patterns in a precise and expressive way.

blocks.textContent: switch in Swift is far more capable than a basic multi-branch statement. It supports rich pattern matching, exhaustive handling, and readable branching for enums, tuples, ranges, and more.

blocks.code:
let statusCode = 404

switch statusCode {
case 200:
    print("OK")
case 404:
    print("Not Found")
default:
    print("Unhandled")
}
blocks.title: Basic switch Statement

blocks.textContent: One of the biggest advantages is exhaustiveness. Swift pushes you to handle every meaningful case, which makes branching safer and especially powerful when working with enums.

topics.title: for-in
topics.summary: Iterates over sequences such as arrays, dictionaries, ranges, and strings.

blocks.textContent: for-in is Swift's main looping construct for iterating over sequences. It reads cleanly, works across many collection types, and usually communicates intent better than index-driven loops.

blocks.title: Looping Over a Collection

blocks.textContent: It also works naturally with ranges, dictionaries, and any custom type that conforms to Sequence. In practice, that makes it the default loop you reach for most of the time.

topics.title: while
topics.summary: Repeats work while a condition remains true, including repeat-while for post-check loops.

blocks.textContent: while is useful when iteration depends on a changing condition rather than walking through a known sequence. It is a better fit for state-driven repetition, retries, and loops that stop when some runtime condition is satisfied.

blocks.title: Basic while Loop

blocks.textContent: When the body must run at least once, Swift also offers repeat-while. The main design question is simple: should the condition be checked before the first iteration or after it?

topics.title: guard
topics.summary: Exits early when required conditions are not met, keeping the main path flat and readable.

blocks.textContent: guard is one of Swift's most practical readability tools. It lets you validate preconditions early, exit when something is missing or invalid, and keep the successful path unindented.

blocks.title: Early Exit with guard

blocks.textContent: That shape makes code easier to scan because failure cases are handled upfront. It is especially effective for optionals, validation rules, and permission checks.

topics.title: defer
topics.summary: Schedules cleanup work to run when the current scope exits.

blocks.textContent: defer lets you declare cleanup logic near the code that sets a resource up, while still guaranteeing that the cleanup runs when the current scope exits. This is especially useful when multiple return paths exist.

blocks.title: Basic defer

blocks.textContent: The value of defer is not just cleanup itself, but reliability. It reduces the chance of forgetting to undo work when functions become more complex and start returning from multiple branches.

topics.title: Pattern Matching
topics.summary: Matches values against structural patterns rather than only comparing for equality.

blocks.textContent: Pattern matching is one of Swift's most expressive language features. Instead of asking only whether two values are equal, Swift can match shapes, associated values, ranges, tuples, and partial structure directly.

blocks.title: Matching Tuple Shapes

blocks.textContent: This is why switch feels unusually powerful in Swift. It is not just branching on fixed values, but describing the form of the data you expect to handle.

topics.title: if case
topics.summary: Uses pattern matching in a single conditional branch without a full switch.

blocks.textContent: if case is the lightweight form of pattern matching. It is useful when you only care about a single matching case and do not need the full structure of a switch statement.

blocks.title: Matching One Case

blocks.textContent: It reads well when the match is short and highly local. If several cases matter, switch is usually the better tool because it keeps all branches visible in one place.

topics.title: guard case
topics.summary: Validates a pattern match up front and exits early when it fails.

blocks.textContent: guard case combines early exit with pattern matching. It is especially useful when the rest of a function only makes sense for one specific enum case or value shape.

blocks.title: Early Exit with Pattern Matching

blocks.textContent: This keeps the happy path clean in the same way guard let does, but with richer structural matching. It is a small construct that often makes enum-heavy code feel much more direct.

topics.title: Optionals
topics.summary: Represents a value that may be present or may be nil.

blocks.textContent: Optionals are one of Swift's defining features. They model absence explicitly, which means code has to acknowledge when a value may not exist instead of quietly hoping for the best.

blocks.code:
var username: String? = "Thiago"
var nickname: String? = nil
blocks.title: Declaring Optionals

blocks.textContent: That explicitness is what makes optionals valuable. They push uncertainty to the surface, which makes APIs safer and control flow more honest about what can actually happen at runtime.

topics.title: Optional Binding
topics.summary: Safely unwraps optional values using if let or guard let.

blocks.textContent: Optional binding is the everyday way to work with optionals safely. Instead of forcing a value out, you ask Swift to continue only when the value is actually there.

blocks.title: if let Binding

blocks.textContent: if let is useful for local branching, while guard let is usually better when the unwrapped value is needed for the rest of the scope. Both express the same safety principle, but with different control-flow shapes.

blocks.title: guard let Binding

topics.title: Optional Chaining
topics.summary: Accesses properties, methods, and subscripts through optional values without crashing.

blocks.textContent: Optional chaining lets you keep moving through a path of values even when one link may be nil. If any step fails, the whole expression becomes nil instead of crashing.

blocks.title: Accessing Through an Optional

blocks.textContent: This is especially useful when working with nested models from APIs, optional relationships, or UI state that is loaded gradually. It keeps the code compact without losing safety.

topics.title: Nil Coalescing
topics.summary: Provides a fallback value when an optional evaluates to nil using ??.

blocks.textContent: The nil-coalescing operator gives you a concise fallback when an optional is nil. It is one of the most practical tools for turning optional values into usable values at the edge of a UI or API boundary.

blocks.code:
let nickname: String? = nil
let displayName = nickname ?? "Guest"
blocks.title: Fallback with ??

blocks.textContent: It reads well when the default value is simple and obvious. If the fallback is expensive, complex, or semantically important, a fuller if or guard can communicate the decision more clearly.

topics.title: Error
topics.summary: Marks a type as something that can represent an error in Swift's throwing system.

blocks.textContent: Error is the protocol behind Swift's native error handling system. Most real code models errors with enums, because enums make failure states explicit and easy to switch over later.

blocks.title: Defining a Custom Error

blocks.textContent: Good error types do more than mark failure. They shape how recoverable cases, developer mistakes, and user-facing messages are separated across the system.

topics.title: throw
topics.summary: Signals an error from a throwing function so the caller must handle or propagate it.

blocks.textContent: throw is how a function reports that it cannot complete successfully. Instead of inventing custom sentinel values or booleans, Swift lets failure move through the call stack in a first-class way.

blocks.title: Throwing an Error

blocks.textContent: The key idea is that throwing does not decide how the error will be handled. It only declares that normal execution cannot continue from that point.

topics.title: do-catch
topics.summary: Executes throwing code and handles matching errors locally.

blocks.textContent: do-catch is the local handling side of Swift error propagation. It is where you attempt throwing work and decide what to do when specific failures happen.

blocks.title: Handling a Thrown Error

blocks.textContent: The basic form catches any error, but Swift also lets you match specific cases. That becomes especially useful when some errors are recoverable and others should simply bubble up or be logged.

topics.title: try?
topics.summary: Turns a throwing expression into an optional, yielding nil if an error occurs.

blocks.textContent: try? is the compact way to say: if this throws, I am willing to treat the failure as nil. It is useful when the exact error does not matter and absence is an acceptable outcome.

blocks.code: let image = try? loadImage(named: "avatar")
blocks.title: Converting Failure to nil

blocks.textContent: The tradeoff is lost error detail. That is acceptable in some convenience paths, but it is the wrong choice when debugging, analytics, or user-facing recovery depends on the actual failure reason.

topics.title: try!
topics.summary: Asserts that a throwing expression will not fail and crashes if that assumption is wrong.

blocks.textContent: try! is the force-unwrapped version of throwing code. It tells Swift that failure is impossible or already guaranteed away, and it will trap if that assumption turns out to be false.

blocks.code: let url = try! validatedURL(from: "https://swift.org")
blocks.title: Force-Trying a Throwing Call

blocks.textContent: That makes it appropriate only when the invariant is truly under your control, such as trusted test fixtures or hardcoded values that have already been validated. In most app logic, regular handling is safer.

topics.title: Result
topics.summary: Represents either a success value or a failure value in a single enum.

blocks.textContent: Result packages success and failure into a single value. It is especially useful in asynchronous APIs, callback-based code, and situations where you want to pass failure around as data instead of throwing immediately.

blocks.title: Using Result in a Completion Handler

blocks.textContent: The important distinction is that throws models control flow, while Result models data. Both are useful, but they serve different architectural shapes.

topics.title: Array
topics.summary: Stores ordered values of the same type and preserves insertion order.

blocks.textContent: Array is Swift's go-to collection for ordered data. It is a natural fit when order matters, duplicates are allowed, and indexed access is useful.

blocks.code:
var frameworks = ["SwiftUI", "UIKit"]
frameworks.append("Combine")
print(frameworks[0])
blocks.title: Creating and Mutating an Array

blocks.textContent: Because arrays are so common, the real design question is often whether order is meaningful. If it is not, Set or Dictionary may express the model better.

topics.title: Set
topics.summary: Stores unique values of the same type without preserving insertion order.

blocks.textContent: Set is built for uniqueness. It answers a different question than Array: not what comes first, but whether a value exists at all and whether duplicates should be collapsed.

blocks.code:
var tags: Set<String> = ["swift", "ios"]
tags.insert("swiftui")
tags.insert("swift")
blocks.title: Creating a Set

blocks.textContent: That makes Set a good choice for membership checks, selected IDs, unique tags, and permission groups. If order becomes important later, it is usually a sign that Array was the better abstraction all along.

topics.title: Dictionary
topics.summary: Stores key-value pairs for fast lookup by key.

blocks.textContent: Dictionary stores associations between keys and values. It is the right tool when access is driven by identity or lookup rather than by position.

blocks.code:
var scores = ["Ana": 10, "Bruno": 8]
scores["Carla"] = 9
let anaScore = scores["Ana"]
blocks.title: Working with Key-Value Pairs

blocks.textContent: A dictionary lookup returns an optional because the key may not exist. That means dictionaries connect naturally with optional binding and nil coalescing in real code.

topics.title: Range
topics.summary: Represents an interval of values and appears throughout iteration, slicing, and APIs with bounds.

blocks.textContent: Range is both a concrete type and a recurring idea across Swift. It represents an interval of values and appears in loops, collection slices, and APIs that need precise boundaries.

blocks.code:
let closed = 1...5
let halfOpen = 0..<5
blocks.title: Closed and Half-Open Ranges

blocks.textContent: The practical difference is boundary inclusion. That small distinction matters a lot when indexing collections or expressing counts, which is why Range is worth understanding as a type, not just an operator result.

topics.title: Slice
topics.summary: Represents a view into part of a collection without eagerly copying all elements.

blocks.textContent: A slice is a view into a portion of another collection. It lets Swift expose part of the original data without necessarily copying everything into a brand-new collection immediately.

blocks.code:
let values = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
let subset = values[1...3]
print(subset)
blocks.title: Slicing an Array

blocks.textContent: This is useful for efficiency, but it also means the result may not behave exactly like a fresh Array in every context. When a concrete array is required, convert intentionally instead of assuming the slice already is one.

topics.title: Functions
topics.summary: Groups reusable behavior with named parameters, return values, and clear call sites.

blocks.textContent: Functions are the basic unit of reusable behavior in Swift. They let you name work, define inputs and outputs, and shape APIs that read clearly at the call site.

blocks.title: Declaring a Function

blocks.textContent: Good function design in Swift is not just about extracting code. It is about choosing names, parameters, and return types that make the call read like a precise description of intent.

topics.title: Argument Labels
topics.summary: Separates how parameters are named inside a function from how they read at the call site.

blocks.textContent: Argument labels are one of the main reasons Swift APIs read so naturally. They let a function expose parameters in a way that makes the call site feel descriptive instead of positional and cryptic.

blocks.title: Using Argument Labels

blocks.textContent: Swift also lets you omit an external label with _. That can be useful when the method name already carries the full meaning, but the default should be readability, not minimal syntax.

topics.title: Variadic Parameters
topics.summary: Accepts zero or more values of the same type as a single parameter.

blocks.textContent: Variadic parameters let a function accept a flexible number of values without forcing the caller to create an array manually. They are useful when the call site should feel lightweight and list-like.

blocks.title: A Variadic Function

blocks.textContent: Inside the function, the variadic parameter is available as an array. That means the feature mainly changes call-site ergonomics, not the underlying model of how the values are processed.

topics.title: inout
topics.summary: Allows a function to modify a caller-provided value directly.

blocks.textContent: inout gives a function temporary write access to a value supplied by the caller. It is Swift's explicit way of saying that the function is allowed to mutate an input rather than just consume it.

blocks.title: Mutating an Argument

blocks.textContent: The ampersand at the call site is important because it makes mutation explicit. That visibility keeps side effects from feeling hidden, which is one of the main design strengths of Swift's approach.

topics.title: Closures
topics.summary: Encapsulates behavior as a value that can be stored, passed, and executed later.

blocks.textContent: Closures are self-contained blocks of behavior. In Swift, they are everywhere: sorting, mapping, async callbacks, task definitions, SwiftUI actions, and many APIs that need flexible custom behavior.

blocks.title: A Closure Value

blocks.textContent: The important mental model is that a closure is not just syntax. It is a real value with a type, which means it can move through your program like any other piece of data.

topics.title: Trailing Closures
topics.summary: Moves a closure argument outside the parentheses for cleaner call-site syntax.

blocks.textContent: Trailing closure syntax is one of Swift's signature readability features. When the final argument is a closure, moving it outside the parentheses often makes the call feel more like a structured block of behavior.

blocks.code:
let numbers = [1, 2, 3]
let doubled = numbers.map { number in
    number * 2
}
blocks.title: Trailing Closure Syntax

blocks.textContent: This matters most in APIs that revolve around behavior, such as sorting, async completion, and SwiftUI builders. The goal is not shorter syntax by itself, but code that reads with a better visual rhythm.

topics.title: Escaping Closures
topics.summary: Marks a closure that can outlive the function call that receives it.

blocks.textContent: An escaping closure is one that may be stored and executed after the surrounding function has returned. Swift requires this to be explicit because lifetime and capture behavior become much more important in that case.

blocks.title: An Escaping Closure Parameter

blocks.textContent: This is the point where capture lists, memory management, and async architecture start to matter. Escaping closures are powerful, but they are also where accidental retain cycles commonly appear.

topics.title: Autoclosure
topics.summary: Automatically wraps an expression in a closure so the caller can use simpler syntax.

blocks.textContent: Autoclosure is a convenience feature that turns an expression into a closure automatically. It is useful when an API wants deferred evaluation but still wants the call site to stay simple.

blocks.title: Using @autoclosure

blocks.textContent: Like several advanced conveniences in Swift, the question is not whether it works, but whether the API remains obvious. Autoclosure is best when deferred behavior feels natural rather than surprising.

topics.title: Struct
topics.summary: Defines a value type that groups related data and behavior.

blocks.textContent: Struct is one of Swift's core modeling tools. It defines a value type, which means copies are independent by default and the model tends to be easier to reason about in state-driven code.

blocks.title: Defining a Struct

blocks.textContent: In modern Swift, structs are usually the default choice for domain models, view state, configuration, and many kinds of plain data. They shine when identity is less important than clear value semantics.

topics.title: Class
topics.summary: Defines a reference type that supports shared identity and inheritance.

blocks.textContent: Class defines a reference type. Unlike structs, class instances are shared through references, which makes identity, lifetime, and mutation behavior much more central to the design.

blocks.title: Defining a Class

blocks.textContent: Classes are a better fit when shared mutable state, object identity, interoperability with Objective-C frameworks, or inheritance are genuinely part of the problem. They are powerful, but they also carry more behavioral weight.

topics.title: Enum
topics.summary: Defines a finite set of related cases with strong pattern-matching support.

blocks.textContent: Enums in Swift are much richer than simple integer-backed constants. They are a serious modeling tool for representing finite states, actions, directions, results, and other closed sets of possibilities.

blocks.title: Defining an Enum

blocks.textContent: Their real strength comes from exhaustiveness and pattern matching. When a model is genuinely one of several known states, an enum often describes it more honestly than booleans or loosely related strings.

topics.title: Raw Values
topics.summary: Associates each enum case with a fixed underlying value such as a string or integer.

blocks.textContent: Raw values let enum cases map to fixed underlying values. This is useful when the enum needs a stable external representation, such as persisted strings, numeric codes, or interoperability with APIs.

blocks.title: Enum Raw Values

blocks.textContent: The key distinction is that raw values are fixed and predefined. They describe a case from the outside, not additional per-instance data carried by the case itself.

topics.title: Associated Values
topics.summary: Stores extra data alongside a specific enum case.

blocks.textContent: Associated values let an enum case carry context-specific data. This is what turns enums from a simple list of names into a genuinely expressive modeling tool.

blocks.title: Enum Cases with Data

blocks.textContent: Once associated values exist, pattern matching becomes the natural way to work with the enum. That combination is one of the strongest language design features in Swift.

topics.title: Initializers
topics.summary: Creates valid instances by assigning initial state through init methods.

blocks.textContent: Initializers define how a type becomes valid and ready to use. In Swift, initialization is strict on purpose: every stored property must be initialized before the instance can escape into the program.

blocks.title: Custom Initializer

blocks.textContent: That makes init more than boilerplate. It is where defaults, validation, and invariants often come together to protect the model from entering invalid states.

topics.title: Subscripts
topics.summary: Provides custom indexed access syntax for your own types.

blocks.textContent: Subscripts let a type expose bracket-based access like collections do. They are a way to make custom models feel natural when indexed or keyed access is central to their API.

blocks.title: Defining a Subscript

blocks.textContent: Subscripts are best when the access pattern is obvious and repeated often. If the intent is less direct, a named method usually communicates meaning better than inventing bracket syntax.

topics.title: Stored Properties
topics.summary: Persists values directly as part of an instance or type.

blocks.textContent: Stored properties are the properties that actually hold data in memory. They form the core state of a struct or class and are usually the first thing to think about when modeling a type.

blocks.title: Basic Stored Properties

blocks.textContent: They are ideal for durable state. If a value can always be derived from other values, a computed property is often a better fit than storing redundant data.

topics.title: Computed Properties
topics.summary: Calculates a value on demand instead of storing it directly.

blocks.textContent: Computed properties derive their value from other state. They are useful when the property should feel like part of the model's interface, even though it is really calculated behavior.

blocks.title: A Read-Only Computed Property

blocks.textContent: The benefit is clarity at the call site. The caution is hidden cost: if computing the value is expensive or surprising, a method may be a more honest API than a property.

topics.title: Lazy Properties
topics.summary: Delays initialization until the property is accessed for the first time.

blocks.textContent: Lazy properties postpone setup until the value is actually needed. That can be useful when initialization is expensive or when some instances may never touch the property at all.

blocks.title: A Lazy Property

blocks.textContent: The gain is deferred cost. The tradeoff is that initialization no longer happens at object creation time, so lifecycle assumptions should stay explicit.

topics.title: Property Observers
topics.summary: Responds to changes in a property's value with willSet and didSet.

blocks.textContent: Property observers let you react when a stored property changes. They are useful for synchronization, logging, lightweight side effects, and enforcing small local rules around mutation.

blocks.title: Using didSet

blocks.textContent: They are best for local reactions that stay close to the property itself. Once the logic grows larger or more architectural, moving it into a method or dedicated abstraction usually scales better.

topics.title: Type Properties
topics.summary: Belongs to the type itself rather than to a specific instance.

blocks.textContent: Type properties are shared at the type level instead of being stored per instance. They are useful for constants, counters, configuration, and behavior that describes the type as a whole.

blocks.title: A Static Property

blocks.textContent: The important design question is whether the data describes one instance or every instance. If the answer is 'every instance', a type property is usually the clearer home.

topics.title: Instance Methods
topics.summary: Defines behavior that operates on a specific instance of a type.

blocks.textContent: Instance methods are functions attached to a particular instance. They are where behavior and state meet, which makes them central to how types express responsibility.

blocks.title: An Instance Method

blocks.textContent: A good instance method usually reads as something the instance can do, not just a random helper function placed nearby. That distinction improves API design more than it first appears.

topics.title: Type Methods
topics.summary: Defines behavior on the type itself using static or class methods.

blocks.textContent: Type methods belong to the type itself rather than a specific instance. They are useful for factories, shared utilities, and behavior that conceptually describes the type as a whole.

blocks.title: A Static Method

blocks.textContent: This is often the right place for creation logic that should not require a half-constructed instance first. In classes, class methods also support overriding when polymorphism matters.

topics.title: Mutating Methods
topics.summary: Marks methods on value types that are allowed to change self or its properties.

blocks.textContent: In value types like structs and enums, methods must be marked mutating if they change the instance. Swift makes this explicit so mutation remains visible in the type's API surface.

blocks.title: A Mutating Method

blocks.textContent: That explicit marker reinforces one of Swift's core ideas: value semantics are important, but mutation is still allowed when it is clearly declared and intentional.

topics.title: Protocol
topics.summary: Defines a shared contract of properties, methods, and requirements that types can adopt.

blocks.textContent: Protocols define capabilities without committing to a specific concrete type. They are one of Swift's main tools for abstraction, composition, and API design based on behavior rather than inheritance.

blocks.title: Defining a Protocol

blocks.textContent: The important shift is conceptual: a protocol says what something can do, not what it is. That makes models easier to swap, test, and compose when responsibilities evolve.

topics.title: Extensions
topics.summary: Adds new behavior to an existing type without modifying its original declaration.

blocks.textContent: Extensions let you add functionality to existing types in a focused way. They are useful for organizing code, grouping related behavior, and adapting system types without subclassing or rewriting them.

blocks.title: Extending an Existing Type

blocks.textContent: Used well, extensions improve structure by keeping related behavior together. Used carelessly, they can scatter a type's meaning across files, so cohesion still matters.

topics.title: Protocol Extensions
topics.summary: Provides default behavior and shared implementation for types that conform to a protocol.

blocks.textContent: Protocol extensions let protocols carry implementation, not just requirements. This is one of Swift's most influential design features because it enables reusable behavior without forcing a class hierarchy.

blocks.title: Default Behavior in a Protocol Extension

blocks.textContent: The power here is real, but so is the subtlety. When default behavior becomes complex, it is important that conforming types still read predictably and do not hide critical semantics behind too much magic.

topics.title: Associated Type
topics.summary: Declares a placeholder type inside a protocol that conforming types must define.

blocks.textContent: Associated types let a protocol describe relationships between types without hardcoding a single concrete choice. They are central to how protocols model abstraction in collections, sequences, and many generic APIs.

blocks.title: Declaring an Associated Type

blocks.textContent: The protocol is not saying what the type must be, only that the type exists and must be used consistently across the contract. That flexibility is what makes associated types so powerful.

topics.title: Generic Functions
topics.summary: Writes functions that work across multiple concrete types while preserving type safety.

blocks.textContent: Generic functions let behavior stay reusable without throwing away type information. Instead of writing one function per type, you can describe the shared shape of the operation once.

blocks.title: A Generic Function

blocks.textContent: The key benefit is that the caller still gets concrete types back. This is what makes generics safer and more expressive than falling back to Any too early.

topics.title: Generic Types
topics.summary: Defines reusable types that can be parameterized by one or more other types.

blocks.textContent: Generic types let you create containers and abstractions that remain strongly typed while adapting to different concrete data. Array, Optional, and Result are all familiar examples of this pattern.

blocks.title: A Generic Type

blocks.textContent: This keeps the model reusable without giving up static guarantees. The result is often cleaner than duplicating types or storing values as loosely typed placeholders.

topics.title: Generic Constraints
topics.summary: Restricts generic types to those that satisfy specific protocol or inheritance requirements.

blocks.textContent: Generic constraints make generics more precise. They let you say not just 'any type', but 'any type that supports this specific capability'.

blocks.title: Constraining a Generic Type

blocks.textContent: This is where generic code stops being vague and starts becoming truly useful. Constraints express the minimum guarantees the implementation needs in order to remain type-safe.

topics.title: some
topics.summary: Hides a concrete return type while preserving that it is still one specific type.

blocks.textContent: some introduces an opaque type. It lets an API hide the exact concrete type while still preserving that the value is one specific, consistent type known by the compiler.

blocks.title: Returning an Opaque Type

blocks.textContent: The important distinction is that some still describes a concrete underlying type. It hides the choice from callers, but it does not mean 'any value that conforms'.

topics.title: any
topics.summary: Represents an existential value that can hold any type conforming to a protocol.

blocks.textContent: any introduces an existential type. It means the value may be one of many conforming concrete types, and that flexibility comes with different tradeoffs than opaque types or generics.

blocks.code: let item: any CustomStringConvertible = 42
blocks.title: Using an Existential Value

blocks.textContent: This is useful when heterogeneity really matters, but it is not a free replacement for generics. The choice between generic, some, and any is really a choice about API shape and guarantees.

topics.title: Type Casting
topics.summary: Checks and converts types at runtime when values move across inheritance or protocol boundaries.

blocks.textContent: Type casting is how Swift lets you inspect or convert runtime types when static knowledge is not enough. It becomes relevant when working with inheritance, protocol-typed values, or heterogeneous collections.

blocks.code: let items: [Any] = [1, "Swift", true]
blocks.title: A Heterogeneous Collection

blocks.textContent: Most of the time, needing casts is a sign that the static model has widened. That is not automatically wrong, but it is worth noticing because it changes how much the compiler can guarantee for you.

topics.title: is
topics.summary: Checks whether a value is of a given type at runtime.

blocks.textContent: The is operator answers a type-checking question without actually converting the value. It is useful when control flow depends on the runtime type, but you do not yet need to access type-specific members.

blocks.code:
let value: Any = "Swift"
let isString = value is String
blocks.title: Checking a Runtime Type

blocks.textContent: It often appears in switch or if branches, but if you actually need the typed value afterward, as? is usually the more practical tool because it combines checking and conversion.

topics.title: as
topics.summary: Performs an unconditional upcast or bridges values when the conversion is guaranteed.

blocks.textContent: The as operator is used for conversions that are already known to be valid, such as upcasting to a superclass or bridging in contexts where the conversion is guaranteed.

blocks.title: A Guaranteed Cast

blocks.textContent: This is not the risky cast. It is the operator you use when the type system already knows the conversion is safe, and you just need to state it explicitly.

topics.title: as?
topics.summary: Attempts a conditional downcast and returns nil if it fails.

blocks.textContent: as? is the safe downcasting operator. It attempts the cast and gives you an optional result, which fits naturally with Swift's broader safety model.

blocks.code:
let value: Any = "Swift"
let text = value as? String
blocks.title: A Conditional Cast

blocks.textContent: This is usually the right choice when success depends on runtime reality. It works especially well with if let, guard let, and pattern matching.

topics.title: as!
topics.summary: Performs a forced downcast and traps at runtime if the cast is invalid.

blocks.textContent: as! is the force-casting operator. It tells Swift that the cast must succeed, and the program will trap if the runtime value does not actually match that assumption.

blocks.code:
let value: Any = "Swift"
let text = value as! String
blocks.title: A Forced Downcast

blocks.textContent: Like force unwrapping and try!, this is only appropriate when the invariant is genuinely guaranteed by surrounding design. Otherwise, it moves uncertainty from the compiler into a runtime crash.

topics.title: KeyPath
topics.summary: References a property path in a type-safe, composable way.

blocks.textContent: Key paths let Swift represent property access as data. Instead of immediately reading a value, you can capture the path to that value in a type-safe form and pass it around.

blocks.title: Creating a Key Path

blocks.textContent: This is especially useful in SwiftUI, Combine, sorting, mapping, and APIs that want declarative access to model fields without using fragile string keys.

topics.title: WritableKeyPath
topics.summary: Represents a key path that can both read and write a property.

blocks.textContent: WritableKeyPath is the mutable form of a key path. It does not just describe where a value lives, but also allows APIs to update that value in a type-safe way.

blocks.title: A Writable Key Path

blocks.textContent: This is valuable in reusable mutation helpers, bindings, and advanced declarative APIs. It keeps property selection strongly typed even when the mutation logic becomes generic.

topics.title: ARC
topics.summary: Automatically manages the lifetime of class instances through reference counting.

blocks.textContent: ARC, or Automatic Reference Counting, is how Swift manages the lifetime of reference types like classes. Instead of asking you to manually retain and release objects, Swift tracks how many strong references still point to an instance.

blocks.title: Reference Counting in Practice

blocks.textContent: Most of the time ARC feels invisible, which is exactly the point. The important moments are when references form cycles or when lifetime assumptions become part of the architecture.

topics.title: Strong Reference Cycles
topics.summary: Occurs when strong references keep objects alive even though they are no longer needed.

blocks.textContent: A strong reference cycle happens when two or more objects keep each other alive through strong references. ARC cannot clean up the cycle because the reference count never reaches zero.

blocks.title: A Potential Reference Cycle

blocks.textContent: In app code, these cycles often show up in delegates, closures, parent-child object graphs, and long-lived managers. They are one of the most important runtime behaviors Swift developers need to recognize early.

topics.title: weak
topics.summary: Declares a non-owning reference that automatically becomes nil when the instance is deallocated.

blocks.textContent: weak breaks ownership by saying: this reference should not keep the object alive. If the instance goes away, the weak reference becomes nil automatically.

blocks.title: A Weak Reference

blocks.textContent: This is commonly the right tool for delegates and back-references where the relationship should be observable but not owning. Because weak references are optional, the API also reflects that the target may disappear.

topics.title: unowned
topics.summary: Declares a non-owning reference that is expected to remain valid for its lifetime.

blocks.textContent: unowned also avoids ownership, but unlike weak it does not become nil. It is for relationships where the referenced instance is expected to outlive or match the lifetime of the holder.

blocks.title: An Unowned Reference

blocks.textContent: That assumption makes unowned more lightweight than weak, but also more dangerous when the lifetime guarantee is wrong. If the object disappears first, accessing the unowned reference traps at runtime.

topics.title: Value Types vs Reference Types
topics.summary: Contrasts copy-based value semantics with shared identity-based reference semantics.

blocks.textContent: One of Swift's deepest design ideas is the distinction between value types and reference types. Structs and enums are copied as values, while classes are shared through references.

blocks.title: Value Semantics in Practice

blocks.textContent: This choice affects mutability, ownership, testing, concurrency, and mental model. Many architectural decisions in Swift are really decisions about whether a problem should be modeled as a value or an identity-bearing object.

topics.title: async / await
topics.summary: Expresses asynchronous code in a direct style without nested completion handlers.

blocks.textContent: async and await are the foundation of Swift's modern concurrency model. They let asynchronous work read more like normal sequential code while still preserving suspension points explicitly.

blocks.title: Calling an Async Function

blocks.textContent: The key design benefit is not just cleaner syntax, but explicit suspension. When code can pause and resume later, Swift makes that visible in the function signature and at the call site.

topics.title: Task
topics.summary: Creates and manages asynchronous units of work in Swift concurrency.

blocks.textContent: Task represents an asynchronous unit of work. It is how code enters the structured concurrency world from synchronous contexts or launches additional concurrent work intentionally.

blocks.title: Starting a Task

blocks.textContent: The design question is not whether you can create a task, but whether the work should be structured, scoped, and cancellable in a way that still makes sense to the rest of the program.

topics.title: TaskGroup
topics.summary: Runs multiple child tasks concurrently and collects their results in a structured scope.

blocks.textContent: Task groups are for dynamic structured concurrency. They let you spawn multiple child tasks, await their results, and keep all of that work tied to a clear parent scope.

blocks.title: Using a Task Group

blocks.textContent: The strength of a task group is that concurrency remains explicit and bounded. You get parallelism, but still inside a structure the compiler and runtime can help manage.

topics.title: actor
topics.summary: Protects mutable state by isolating access behind the actor's concurrency boundary.

blocks.textContent: Actors are Swift's answer to protecting mutable shared state in concurrent code. They isolate state so that access happens in a controlled way instead of through uncontrolled data races.

blocks.title: Defining an Actor

blocks.textContent: This is not just a new keyword, but a different ownership model for concurrency. It shifts the question from 'who locks this?' to 'who is allowed to touch this state at all?'

topics.title: MainActor
topics.summary: Pins code to the main actor so UI-related state stays on the main execution context.

blocks.textContent: MainActor is the concurrency tool that expresses main-thread-like UI isolation in Swift's actor model. It is how Swift makes 'this belongs on the main side of the app' explicit in code.

blocks.title: Using @MainActor

blocks.textContent: This matters because UI consistency is not just about convention anymore. Swift can now help enforce isolation rules that used to rely mostly on discipline and review.

topics.title: AsyncSequence
topics.summary: Represents values produced asynchronously over time and consumed with for-await-in.

blocks.textContent: AsyncSequence is the asynchronous counterpart to Sequence. It models values that arrive over time instead of being immediately available all at once.

blocks.title: Consuming an Async Sequence

blocks.textContent: This is a natural fit for streams, events, notifications, and other time-based data flows. It gives asynchronous iteration a first-class place in the language.

topics.title: Cancellation
topics.summary: Lets asynchronous work stop cooperatively when it is no longer needed.

blocks.textContent: Cancellation in Swift concurrency is cooperative. A task is asked to stop, but the code still needs to observe that signal and decide how to wind down responsibly.

blocks.title: Checking for Cancellation

blocks.textContent: This matters for UI-driven code especially, where work often becomes irrelevant before it finishes. Good cancellation support is part of correctness, not just a performance optimization.

topics.title: Property Wrappers
topics.summary: Encapsulates storage behavior and access rules behind a reusable property annotation.

blocks.textContent: Property wrappers package recurring storage behavior behind an annotation-like syntax. They are especially powerful when a property needs extra semantics such as persistence, validation, state management, or indirection.

blocks.title: A Custom Property Wrapper

blocks.textContent: The feature becomes especially visible in SwiftUI, but the deeper idea is reusable property semantics. It is about giving storage behavior a proper abstraction boundary.

topics.title: Result Builders
topics.summary: Transforms block-like syntax into structured values through a custom builder type.

blocks.textContent: Result builders let APIs interpret a block of statements as a structured output. This is one of the key techniques behind SwiftUI's declarative syntax and other builder-style DSLs in Swift.

blocks.title: A Simple Result Builder

blocks.textContent: The attraction is expressive syntax. The tradeoff is indirection: once builders get clever, it becomes important that the transformed model still feels understandable to the people reading the call site.

topics.title: Macros
topics.summary: Generates code at compile time to reduce boilerplate and encode reusable transformations.

blocks.textContent: Macros bring compile-time code generation into modern Swift. They are designed to reduce repetitive boilerplate while keeping the transformation visible and grounded in the language.

blocks.code: #warning("Review this API before release")
blocks.title: A Macro at the Call Site

blocks.textContent: The real promise of macros is not magic, but leverage. They let code generation become deliberate and reviewable instead of hiding behind external scripts or copy-paste patterns.

topics.title: Access Control
topics.summary: Controls which declarations are visible across files, modules, and public APIs.

blocks.textContent: Access control in Swift defines who can see and use a declaration. It is not just about hiding code, but about shaping module boundaries, protecting invariants, and keeping APIs intentional.

blocks.title: Different Access Levels

blocks.textContent: The best default is usually the narrowest visibility that still supports the design. Broad visibility is easy to add later; overexposed APIs are much harder to take back cleanly.

topics.title: Modules and Imports
topics.summary: Organizes code into module boundaries and brings external declarations into scope with import.

blocks.textContent: Modules are the packaging boundary of Swift code, and import is how names from another module become available in the current file. This is the layer where language design meets project structure.

blocks.code:
import Foundation
import SwiftUI
blocks.title: Importing Modules

blocks.textContent: Understanding modules matters because access control, compilation boundaries, testability, and API exposure all depend on where those boundaries are drawn.

topics.title: UIView
topics.summary: The fundamental building block for visual content and interaction in UIKit.

blocks.textContent: UIView is the foundation of UIKit rendering. Labels, buttons, image views, stack views, and most custom interface elements either inherit from it directly or build on top of it.

blocks.title: Basic UIView

blocks.textContent: A UIView manages geometry, drawing, subview hierarchy, and event handling. Frame and bounds still matter conceptually even when Auto Layout is doing the positioning, because they define where the view lives and how its internal coordinate system behaves.

Views form a tree. You compose interfaces by adding subviews, and each view becomes responsible for presenting or containing a piece of the screen. UIKit stays practical when you keep each view's job narrow and explicit.

blocks.code:
let container = UIView()
let badge = UIView()

badge.backgroundColor = .systemBlue
badge.translatesAutoresizingMaskIntoConstraints = false
container.addSubview(badge)

NSLayoutConstraint.activate([
    badge.centerXAnchor.constraint(equalTo: container.centerXAnchor),
    badge.centerYAnchor.constraint(equalTo: container.centerYAnchor),
    badge.widthAnchor.constraint(equalToConstant: 80),
    badge.heightAnchor.constraint(equalToConstant: 80)
])
blocks.title: UIView with Auto Layout

blocks.textContent: Most day-to-day UIView work is about containment and appearance: background color, alpha, clipping, corner radius, transforms, and gesture attachment. For custom rendering, you usually subclass UIView and override lifecycle or drawing methods intentionally rather than stuffing everything into a view controller.

topics.title: UILabel
topics.summary: Displays read-only text using UIKit typography and layout behavior.

blocks.textContent: UILabel is UIKit's standard view for presenting non-editable text. It handles single-line and multiline content, aligns with Dynamic Type and semantic colors, and usually carries the bulk of text rendering in classic UIKit screens.

blocks.code:
let titleLabel = UILabel()
titleLabel.text = "Hello UIKit"
titleLabel.font = .preferredFont(forTextStyle: .title3)
titleLabel.textColor = .label
blocks.title: Basic UILabel

blocks.textContent: UILabel defaults to a single line, which is useful for compact interface chrome but not enough for descriptive content. Setting numberOfLines to 0 allows the label to grow vertically and wrap as needed.

blocks.code:
let bodyLabel = UILabel()
bodyLabel.text = "UIKit labels can expand across multiple lines when the layout allows it."
bodyLabel.numberOfLines = 0
bodyLabel.lineBreakMode = .byWordWrapping
blocks.title: Multiline UILabel

blocks.textContent: When part of a sentence needs different emphasis, attributedText is the practical step up from plain text. It gives you range-based control over font, color, paragraph style, and other text attributes without splitting content into multiple labels.

blocks.title: Attributed UILabel

blocks.textContent: UILabel is simple, but typography choices still matter. preferredFont(forTextStyle:), adjustsFontForContentSizeCategory, alignment, truncation, and semantic colors usually do more for production quality than custom font tricks alone.

topics.title: UIImageView
topics.summary: Displays images and animation frames inside a UIKit view hierarchy.

blocks.textContent: UIImageView is UIKit's dedicated image presentation view. It is commonly used for icons, thumbnails, illustrations, avatars, and decorative backgrounds where the image needs to live naturally inside Auto Layout and the view tree.

blocks.title: Basic UIImageView

blocks.textContent: contentMode is one of the most important properties on UIImageView. It decides whether the image fits, fills, centers, or stretches inside the available bounds, and that choice strongly affects whether the result feels crisp or awkwardly cropped.

UIImageView also works well with SF Symbols. In that case, tintColor, preferredSymbolConfiguration, and rendering mode matter as much as the image itself because symbols behave more like typographic icons than fixed bitmaps.

blocks.title: UIImageView with an SF Symbol

blocks.textContent: For remote images, UIImageView is usually only the rendering endpoint. Loading, caching, placeholder handling, and cancellation belong to the surrounding image pipeline, not to the image view itself.

topics.title: UIStackView
topics.summary: Lays out arranged subviews in a linear horizontal or vertical stack.

blocks.textContent: UIStackView is UIKit's most practical composition tool for common layouts. Instead of creating constraint relationships manually for every sibling view, you describe an axis, spacing, alignment, and distribution, and let the stack manage the arrangement.

blocks.title: Basic UIStackView

blocks.textContent: The key distinction is between subviews and arrangedSubviews. A stack view manages the layout of arranged subviews, but those views are still regular subviews in the hierarchy. That detail matters when hiding, removing, or animating content.

axis, alignment, distribution, and spacing do most of the real work. Once you understand those four levers, UIStackView becomes the fastest way to build settings rows, cards, forms, onboarding layouts, and lightweight reusable components.

blocks.title: Nested Stack Views

blocks.textContent: Stack views are excellent for structured layouts, but they are not a replacement for every custom arrangement. Once a design depends on overlapping content, precise proportional behavior, or drawing-heavy composition, explicit constraints or custom container views usually become clearer.

topics.title: UIScrollView
topics.summary: Provides scrolling, zooming, and visible viewport management for content larger than its bounds.

blocks.textContent: UIScrollView is the base scrolling container in UIKit. Table views, collection views, and text views all build on top of it, but plain UIScrollView remains useful whenever you need freeform scrollable content or a custom composed screen.

blocks.title: Basic UIScrollView

blocks.textContent: With Auto Layout, the modern mental model is contentLayoutGuide plus frameLayoutGuide. You constrain the content to the content layout guide, and constrain width or height relative to the frame layout guide depending on the scrolling direction you want.

blocks.code:
let scrollView = UIScrollView()
let contentView = UIView()

scrollView.translatesAutoresizingMaskIntoConstraints = false
contentView.translatesAutoresizingMaskIntoConstraints = false
scrollView.addSubview(contentView)

NSLayoutConstraint.activate([
    contentView.topAnchor.constraint(equalTo: scrollView.contentLayoutGuide.topAnchor),
    contentView.bottomAnchor.constraint(equalTo: scrollView.contentLayoutGuide.bottomAnchor),
    contentView.leadingAnchor.constraint(equalTo: scrollView.contentLayoutGuide.leadingAnchor),
    contentView.trailingAnchor.constraint(equalTo: scrollView.contentLayoutGuide.trailingAnchor),
    contentView.widthAnchor.constraint(equalTo: scrollView.frameLayoutGuide.widthAnchor)
])
blocks.title: Scroll Content with Layout Guides

blocks.textContent: Inset adjustment, bouncing, paging, keyboard dismissal, and indicator visibility are usually more important than raw scrolling itself. UIScrollView feels polished when these behaviors match the content and interaction style of the screen.

UIScrollView can also zoom, but that path depends on its delegate and a viewForZooming implementation. It is powerful for image viewers and canvas-like interfaces, though unnecessary for most everyday forms and settings screens.

topics.title: UIControl
topics.summary: The base class for interactive controls that send actions in response to user events.

blocks.textContent: UIControl sits between plain UIView and concrete controls like UIButton, UISwitch, and UISlider. It adds the event-driven behavior that makes UIKit controls feel consistent: highlighted, selected, enabled, tracking, and target-action delivery.

The core idea is target-action. A control emits events such as touchUpInside or valueChanged, and another object handles the response. This keeps interaction wiring lightweight without forcing every control to define its own callback API from scratch.

blocks.title: Subclassing UIControl

blocks.textContent: Control state is as important as the event itself. enabled, selected, highlighted, and focused are part of how UIKit communicates interaction feedback, and custom controls usually become clearer once they model those states deliberately.

blocks.code: sendActions(for: .valueChanged)
blocks.title: Emitting a Control Event

blocks.textContent: You do not instantiate UIControl directly very often, but understanding it explains why UIKit input components behave the way they do. It is the conceptual base for interaction in classic UIKit APIs.

topics.title: UIButton
topics.summary: A control that triggers app-defined actions when people tap it.

blocks.textContent: UIButton is UIKit's standard action control. It combines a tappable surface, built-in interaction feedback, control states, and flexible content configuration for titles, images, symbols, and modern button styles.

blocks.title: Basic UIButton

blocks.textContent: Classic UIKit wiring uses addTarget with a selector. That pattern is still common and worth knowing because it appears across the entire framework, not just in buttons.

blocks.code: button.addTarget(self, action: #selector(saveTapped), for: .touchUpInside)
blocks.title: Connecting a Button Action

blocks.textContent: Modern UIKit increasingly favors UIButton.Configuration. It gives buttons a more declarative shape for title, subtitle, image, padding, and background styling, which is usually cleaner than stitching appearance together through many state-specific setters.

blocks.title: UIButton with Configuration

blocks.textContent: Like most good controls, a button is mainly about clarity of intent and state. Title, image, enabled state, configuration, and surrounding layout usually matter more than custom decoration if the goal is a clean, trustworthy action surface.

topics.title: UISwitch
topics.summary: A control that represents and changes a single on or off value.

blocks.textContent: UISwitch is UIKit's binary toggle control. It is best for settings and preferences where the user is enabling or disabling a behavior rather than choosing among several options.

blocks.title: Basic UISwitch

blocks.textContent: UISwitch emits .valueChanged when its state changes. In practice, that means the control is easy to connect to a model or preference store without inventing custom event plumbing.

blocks.code: notificationsSwitch.addTarget(self, action: #selector(switchChanged(_:)), for: .valueChanged)
blocks.title: Observing State Changes

blocks.textContent: The control works best when the label next to it clearly describes the resulting state. Good toggle design is usually less about the switch itself and more about whether the surrounding copy removes ambiguity.
You can also update the switch programmatically with setOn(_:animated:), which is useful when the backing state changes from elsewhere and the interface should reflect that transition visibly.

topics.title: UISlider
topics.summary: A control for selecting a single value from a continuous range.

blocks.textContent: UISlider lets people adjust a value along a linear track. It is a strong fit for things like volume, intensity, opacity, playback position, or any setting where approximate adjustment is more natural than typing a number.

blocks.code:
let slider = UISlider()
slider.minimumValue = 0
slider.maximumValue = 100
slider.value = 40
blocks.title: Basic UISlider

blocks.textContent: minimumValue, maximumValue, and value are the essential properties. Once those are explicit, the control becomes predictable both for the user and for the rest of your code that consumes the selected value.

blocks.code: slider.addTarget(self, action: #selector(sliderChanged(_:)), for: .valueChanged)
blocks.title: Reading Slider Changes

blocks.textContent: UISlider can update continuously while the thumb moves, which is great for live previews but sometimes too chatty for expensive work. In those cases, you usually debounce the side effects or wait for a more deliberate interaction boundary.
Track tint, thumb image, and minimum or maximum images let you tune the control visually, but the main UX responsibility is still communicating what the range means and whether exact values matter.

topics.title: UISegmentedControl
topics.summary: A horizontal control made of multiple segments for switching between closely related options.

blocks.textContent: UISegmentedControl is designed for a small set of mutually exclusive choices that all deserve equal prominence. It works well when users need to switch modes, filters, or representations without leaving the current context.

blocks.code:
let segment = UISegmentedControl(items: ["Day", "Week", "Month"])
segment.selectedSegmentIndex = 0
blocks.title: Basic UISegmentedControl

blocks.textContent: Like other UIControl subclasses, segmented controls emit .valueChanged. The selectedSegmentIndex is the simplest way to read the choice, though title or image lookup often gives the code better meaning than raw integer branches.

blocks.code: segment.addTarget(self, action: #selector(segmentChanged(_:)), for: .valueChanged)
blocks.title: Responding to Segment Changes

blocks.textContent: Segmented controls work best when each option is short, stable, and comparable. Once labels become long or the option count grows, a menu, picker, or separate navigation surface usually scales better.

UIKit also supports momentary behavior, where a segment acts more like a button than a persistent selection. That is less common today, but it shows that the control can model either stateful choice or lightweight action grouping depending on context.

topics.title: UITextField
topics.summary: A single-line text input control for forms, search, and lightweight editing.

blocks.textContent: UITextField is UIKit's standard single-line text entry control. It appears in sign-in screens, search bars, payment flows, profile forms, and anywhere the user needs to provide a short value with keyboard support.

blocks.code:
let emailField = UITextField()
emailField.placeholder = "Email"
emailField.borderStyle = .roundedRect
emailField.keyboardType = .emailAddress
blocks.title: Basic UITextField

blocks.textContent: Keyboard type, autocorrection, capitalization, return key style, secure entry, and content type usually matter more than the visual chrome. Good text-field UX comes from reducing friction and making the expected input obvious.
UITextFieldDelegate gives you hooks for validation, return-key handling, editing boundaries, and focus flow. In practice, delegation is where many UIKit forms gain their behavior and coordination.

blocks.code:
emailField.delegate = self
emailField.textContentType = .emailAddress
emailField.autocapitalizationType = .none
emailField.autocorrectionType = .no
blocks.title: Common UITextField Configuration

blocks.textContent: For simpler cases, editingChanged and related control events are often enough. That gives you a lighter alternative to delegates when the goal is just to observe text as it changes or react to submission.

topics.title: UITextView
topics.summary: A scrollable multiline text region for editing or displaying longer content.

blocks.textContent: UITextView is the multiline text component in UIKit. It supports longer editing sessions, selection, scrolling, rich text attributes, and text-system behaviors that go well beyond what a single-line field is meant to handle.

blocks.title: Basic UITextView

blocks.textContent: The practical difference from UITextField is scope. UITextField handles short structured input, while UITextView is for draft-like or document-like content where line breaks, scrolling, and extended editing are expected.

Because UITextView builds on the text system, it also opens the door to attributed text, link detection, text container inset control, and more detailed editing behavior. That makes it much more powerful, but also heavier, than a field.

blocks.code:
notesView.textContainerInset = UIEdgeInsets(top: 12, left: 8, bottom: 12, right: 8)
notesView.isScrollEnabled = true
blocks.title: Adjusting Editing Insets

blocks.textContent: UITextView does not provide a built-in placeholder, so most apps implement that behavior with a companion label or custom wrapper. That small detail comes up often and is one of the first signs that text views usually need a bit more composition around them.

topics.title: UIDatePicker
topics.summary: A control for selecting dates and times using platform-appropriate presentation styles.

blocks.textContent: UIDatePicker wraps date and time selection into a control that stays consistent with platform conventions. It is much safer and more usable than asking people to type raw date strings into a text field.

blocks.title: Basic UIDatePicker

blocks.textContent: Mode and style are the two main decisions. datePickerMode defines what is being chosen, while preferredDatePickerStyle influences how that choice is presented, such as wheels, compact, or inline depending on platform support.

minimumDate, maximumDate, locale, calendar, and minuteInterval help turn the picker into a domain-specific input instead of a generic date surface. That is usually what makes it feel correct in booking, scheduling, and reminder flows.

blocks.code:
datePicker.minimumDate = Date()
