datePicker.minuteInterval = 15
datePicker.addTarget(self, action: #selector(dateChanged(_:)), for: .valueChanged)
blocks.title: Constraining and Observing the Picker

blocks.textContent: The biggest design win is usually not the picker itself, but removing ambiguity about format, timezone expectations, and valid ranges. UIDatePicker is one of UIKit's clearest examples of system controls solving real UX problems better than custom input.

topics.title: UIPickerView
topics.summary: A wheel-based control for choosing values from one or more lists of options.

blocks.textContent: UIPickerView presents options in spinning wheel columns called components. It is useful when the user is selecting from a constrained set of known values and the wheel metaphor fits the interaction better than a menu or full-screen list.
The picker depends on data source and delegate protocols. The data source tells UIKit how many components and rows exist, while the delegate provides titles, custom views, and selection handling.

blocks.title: Basic UIPickerView

blocks.textContent: Multiple components let you model coordinated selections such as duration, unit pairs, or structured category pickers. That said, a picker only stays usable while the option set remains easy to scan in wheel form.

blocks.code:
pickerView.selectRow(2, inComponent: 0, animated: false)
let selectedRow = pickerView.selectedRow(inComponent: 0)
blocks.title: Reading and Setting a Selection

blocks.textContent: UIPickerView is still valuable, but it is not always the first modern choice. If the options are few, a segmented control may be faster. If the options are many or need search, a menu or list-based selection flow is often clearer.

topics.title: UIProgressView
topics.summary: Displays the progress of a task over time using a horizontal track.

blocks.textContent: UIProgressView is UIKit's compact control for showing determinate progress. It works best when the app can actually estimate how much of the work is complete, such as downloads, uploads, imports, or multi-step processing.

blocks.code:
let progressView = UIProgressView(progressViewStyle: .default)
progressView.progress = 0.4
progressView.trackTintColor = .systemGray5
progressView.progressTintColor = .systemBlue
blocks.title: Basic UIProgressView

blocks.textContent: The key idea is that progress is numeric and bounded. UIProgressView is not for generic loading states; it is for moments when you know enough to communicate a meaningful fraction of completion.

blocks.code: progressView.setProgress(0.75, animated: true)
blocks.title: Animating a Progress Update

blocks.textContent: A progress bar becomes more trustworthy when its motion roughly matches the work being done. If the value stalls, jumps erratically, or reaches the end too early, users stop believing it even if the control itself is technically correct.

In real interfaces, UIProgressView often works best when paired with context such as a file name, a percentage label, or a cancel action. The bar alone shows movement, but the surrounding UI explains what the movement means.

topics.title: UIActivityIndicatorView
topics.summary: Shows that work is in progress when the duration or exact completion is unknown.

blocks.textContent: UIActivityIndicatorView is UIKit's indeterminate loading indicator. It tells the user that something is happening, but not how far along it is, which makes it appropriate for short or unpredictable work where precise progress is unavailable.

blocks.title: Basic UIActivityIndicatorView

blocks.textContent: The important distinction from UIProgressView is uncertainty. When the app cannot honestly estimate completion, an activity indicator is usually the more accurate choice because it promises activity without pretending to know the timeline.

hidesWhenStopped keeps the interface cleaner by removing the spinner when the task finishes. That small detail matters because inactive indicators left on screen quickly make an interface feel stale or confusing.

blocks.code: spinner.stopAnimating()
blocks.title: Stopping the Indicator

blocks.textContent: Spinners are best for brief waiting states or embedded loading areas. If the wait might be long, pairing the indicator with a label, a skeleton state, or a more informative status message usually gives users better confidence about what the app is doing.

topics.title: UIRefreshControl
topics.summary: Adds pull-to-refresh behavior to scroll views such as table and collection views.

blocks.textContent: UIRefreshControl provides the familiar pull-to-refresh interaction found throughout iOS. It attaches naturally to scroll-based interfaces and gives users a lightweight, explicit way to ask the app to reload content.

blocks.title: Basic UIRefreshControl

blocks.textContent: The control is tightly tied to scroll interaction. That makes it a natural fit for feeds, lists, inboxes, and dashboards where refreshing content is conceptually connected to revisiting the top of the screen.
The important lifecycle detail is ending the refresh explicitly. If endRefreshing is never called, the interface remains stuck in the loading state even if the new data has already arrived.

blocks.code: refreshControl.endRefreshing()
blocks.title: Ending the Refresh State

blocks.textContent: Pull-to-refresh is a strong pattern when updates are optional and user-driven. If the screen needs aggressive background syncing or automatic invalidation, the refresh control should complement that behavior rather than become the only way data stays current.

topics.title: UITableView
topics.summary: Displays data in a vertically scrolling list of rows and sections.

blocks.textContent: UITableView is UIKit's classic list component. It is optimized for vertically scrolling rows, optional sections, cell reuse, and interaction patterns such as selection, swipe actions, editing, and pull-to-refresh.

blocks.title: Basic UITableView

blocks.textContent: The table view itself does not own your data. It renders what the data source describes, which means the quality of the list often depends more on how clearly the backing model is structured than on the table view API alone.

Cell reuse is one of the component's core ideas. Instead of creating a new row view for every visible item, UITableView recycles cells as they scroll on and off screen, which keeps long lists fast and memory-efficient.

blocks.code: tableView.register(UITableViewCell.self, forCellReuseIdentifier: "Cell")
blocks.title: Registering a Table View Cell

blocks.textContent: UITableView remains the cleanest choice when the interface is fundamentally a vertical list of rows. Once the layout becomes highly irregular, grid-like, or composition-heavy, UICollectionView usually becomes the better foundation.

topics.title: UICollectionView
topics.summary: Displays ordered data using highly flexible layouts, cells, and supplementary views.

blocks.textContent: UICollectionView is UIKit's most flexible list and grid container. It can present rows, grids, carousels, dashboards, and compositional layouts while still relying on reuse, data sources, and cell-based rendering.

blocks.title: Basic UICollectionView

blocks.textContent: The defining feature is layout abstraction. The collection view handles scrolling, selection, and reuse, while the layout object decides where items and supplementary views appear. That separation is what makes the component scale from simple grids to much richer interfaces.

Like table views, collection views rely heavily on cell reuse. The collection itself is infrastructure; the real behavior comes from the relationship between your data model, cell configuration, and layout strategy.

blocks.code: collectionView.register(UICollectionViewCell.self, forCellWithReuseIdentifier: "Cell")
blocks.title: Registering a Collection View Cell

blocks.textContent: Modern UIKit leans heavily on UICollectionView even for interfaces that used to default to UITableView. If the screen may grow in complexity later, starting with collection view infrastructure often gives you more room without forcing a rewrite.

topics.title: UITableViewCell
topics.summary: Represents a reusable row in a table view.

blocks.textContent: UITableViewCell is the reusable row view used by UITableView. It packages content, state, selection behavior, editing affordances, and background styling into a single unit that can be recycled efficiently as the list scrolls.
Modern UIKit encourages contentConfiguration rather than manually juggling textLabel and detailTextLabel. That shift makes cell configuration more explicit and better aligned with evolving list APIs.

blocks.title: Configuring a Modern Table View Cell

blocks.textContent: Because cells are reused, configuration needs to be deterministic. A cell should fully describe itself from the current model state instead of assuming anything left over from a previous item.

Subclassing still makes sense when a row has custom structure or behavior, but many everyday rows now fit well within content configuration, accessories, and background configuration without custom drawing or heavy view hierarchy code.

topics.title: UICollectionViewCell
topics.summary: Represents a reusable item view inside a collection view.

blocks.textContent: UICollectionViewCell is the reusable visual unit of a collection view. It can represent a row-like item, a card, a tile, or almost any other shape, depending on how the collection layout positions and sizes it.

Like table view cells, modern collection view cells benefit from contentConfiguration and backgroundConfiguration. Those APIs reduce the need for ad hoc subview setup in simple and medium-complexity item designs.

blocks.title: Configuring a Collection View Cell

blocks.textContent: Selection, highlighting, background appearance, and model-driven configuration all need to survive reuse cleanly. In practice, collection view cells become robust when you treat them as state renderers rather than tiny controllers.

Custom subclasses are especially common here because collection view layouts often want more distinctive item designs than table rows do. Even then, keeping the cell focused on rendering and not on data ownership keeps the architecture healthier.

topics.title: Diffable Data Source
topics.summary: Applies list and collection updates by diffing snapshots instead of manually coordinating insertions and deletions.

blocks.textContent: Diffable data source modernizes table and collection updates by letting you describe the current state of the UI as a snapshot. UIKit computes the differences and applies insertions, deletions, and moves safely, which removes a large class of synchronization bugs.

The core mental model is identity. Sections and items need stable identifiers so UIKit can understand what changed between one snapshot and the next instead of treating everything as brand-new content.

blocks.code:
var snapshot = NSDiffableDataSourceSnapshot<Section, Item>()
snapshot.appendSections([.main])
snapshot.appendItems(items, toSection: .main)
dataSource.apply(snapshot, animatingDifferences: true)
blocks.title: Applying a Snapshot

blocks.textContent: The biggest gain is not animation polish by itself, but correctness. Instead of manually coordinating beginUpdates, reloads, deletes, and index paths, you describe the state once and let UIKit reconcile it.

Diffable data source is especially valuable when list content changes dynamically due to filtering, network updates, or user edits. It scales better than legacy imperative updates once the screen has any real complexity.

topics.title: Cell Registration
topics.summary: Registers and configures reusable cells with a configuration handler instead of manual dequeue boilerplate.

blocks.textContent: Cell registration is the modern UIKit API for creating and configuring reusable cells. Instead of scattering registration and configuration logic across several methods, you define the cell type and its configuration in one place.

This pattern reduces identifier string mistakes and aligns naturally with diffable data sources. It also makes list code easier to read because the configuration lives close to the data being rendered.

blocks.title: Using UITableView.CellRegistration

blocks.textContent: The same idea exists for collection views, which means your table and collection infrastructure can adopt a similar style. That consistency becomes valuable once a codebase uses both heavily.

Cell registration is one of those APIs that feels small but improves architecture noticeably. It pushes cell setup toward a more declarative, local, and model-driven style, which fits the direction of modern UIKit overall.

topics.title: UIViewController
topics.summary: Manages a screen or a portion of your app's interface and coordinates its lifecycle.

blocks.textContent: UIViewController is the central coordination object of UIKit screens. It manages a view hierarchy, responds to lifecycle events, handles navigation and presentation, and acts as the bridge between interface structure and application logic.

blocks.title: Basic UIViewController

blocks.textContent: The lifecycle methods matter because they define when setup, layout-dependent work, appearance updates, and cleanup should happen. Understanding viewDidLoad, viewWillAppear, viewDidAppear, and related callbacks is part of writing predictable UIKit screens.

A good controller coordinates rather than owns everything. Once it starts becoming the storage location for every subview, network request, validation rule, and formatting concern, the screen quickly becomes harder to evolve.

blocks.title: Adding Views in a View Controller

blocks.textContent: Most UIKit architecture discussions eventually revolve around controllers because they sit at the center of navigation, layout, and interaction. The better their boundaries are, the healthier the whole app tends to be.

topics.title: UINavigationController
topics.summary: Manages a stack of view controllers for hierarchical navigation.

blocks.textContent: UINavigationController is UIKit's standard container for hierarchical drill-down navigation. It keeps a stack of view controllers and handles push and pop transitions, navigation bar presentation, and back navigation behavior.

The important mental model is the stack. Each push adds a new screen on top, and each pop returns to the previous one. That structure matches flows where users move deeper into related detail rather than switching between unrelated sections.

blocks.code:
let root = LibraryViewController()
let navigationController = UINavigationController(rootViewController: root)
blocks.title: Creating a Navigation Controller

blocks.code:
let detailViewController = DetailViewController()
navigationController?.pushViewController(detailViewController, animated: true)
blocks.title: Pushing a View Controller

blocks.textContent: Navigation bars, large titles, back buttons, and bar button items all live naturally in this container. Much of UIKit's screen-to-screen ergonomics depends on using the navigation controller as the place where hierarchy becomes visible to the user.

When the app's structure is clearly hierarchical, a navigation controller tends to feel native almost immediately. Problems usually start when developers try to force unrelated modes or global app sections into a push stack that was never meant for them.

topics.title: UITabBarController
topics.summary: Manages multiple root sections of an app through a tab bar interface.

blocks.textContent: UITabBarController is the container for top-level app sections. Each tab usually represents a distinct root area of the product, such as Home, Search, Library, or Settings, rather than just another step deeper in the same flow.

blocks.title: Basic UITabBarController

blocks.textContent: The conceptual difference from a navigation controller is breadth instead of depth. Tabs switch between peer destinations, while navigation stacks move deeper into one destination's hierarchy.

In practice, each tab is often wrapped in its own UINavigationController. That pattern lets each top-level section keep an independent navigation stack, which is how many classic iOS apps preserve context while users move between tabs.

A tab bar works well when the app really has a few stable primary areas. If the tab set grows too large or changes too frequently, the interface becomes harder to scan and loses the clarity that makes tabs valuable in the first place.

topics.title: UISplitViewController
topics.summary: Presents multiple side-by-side interface columns that adapt across size classes.

blocks.textContent: UISplitViewController is UIKit's adaptive multi-column container. It is especially useful on iPad and larger environments where list-detail, sidebar-detail, or three-column workflows benefit from presenting related context at the same time.

The key idea is adaptivity. A split interface may show multiple columns simultaneously in regular-width environments, but collapse into a more linear navigation experience when space becomes constrained.

blocks.title: Basic UISplitViewController

blocks.textContent: Modern split view APIs make the container more expressive than the older master-detail mental model. Primary, supplementary, and secondary roles let the structure describe sidebar, content, and detail relationships more clearly.

This container is strongest when the product genuinely benefits from simultaneous context. If the app only ever needs one screen at a time, a navigation controller is usually simpler and more coherent.

topics.title: UIPageViewController
topics.summary: Manages navigation between pages of content using gestures or programmatic transitions.

blocks.textContent: UIPageViewController is a container for page-based navigation. It works well for onboarding flows, tutorial steps, magazine-like reading, and experiences where adjacent content is naturally explored by swiping forward or backward.

The container depends on a data source that provides the view controller before and after the current one. That design keeps the page controller focused on transition mechanics while your app remains responsible for page identity and order.

blocks.title: Basic UIPageViewController

blocks.code:
pageViewController.setViewControllers(
    [pages[0]],
    direction: .forward,
    animated: false
)
blocks.title: Setting the Current Page

blocks.textContent: Page-based navigation feels natural when the user expects a bounded sequence. If the content is better understood as a list, a freeform feed, or a hierarchical drill-down, a page controller often becomes the wrong interaction metaphor.

topics.title: Container View Controllers
topics.summary: Compose child view controllers to build custom screen structures and coordination patterns.

blocks.textContent: Container view controllers let you build your own structural controllers by embedding children inside a parent. UINavigationController, UITabBarController, and UISplitViewController are all examples of this pattern applied by UIKit itself.

The parent-child relationship matters because it preserves lifecycle propagation, appearance callbacks, and responder-chain behavior. Simply adding another controller's view as a subview is not enough when you actually want correct containment.

blocks.code:
addChild(childViewController)
view.addSubview(childViewController.view)
childViewController.didMove(toParent: self)
blocks.title: Embedding a Child View Controller

blocks.textContent: Custom containers become useful when a screen has stable regions with their own responsibilities, such as a permanently embedded player, a dashboard made of independent modules, or a split experience that does not quite match the stock containers.

Containment is powerful, but it should stay structural. If it becomes a workaround for unclear ownership or a substitute for simpler composition, the result usually feels heavier than the problem actually requires.

topics.title: Auto Layout
topics.summary: Positions and sizes interface elements by solving relationships instead of assigning frames manually.

blocks.textContent: Auto Layout is UIKit's constraint-based layout system. Instead of calculating every frame directly, you describe relationships between views and let the engine solve a layout that adapts to screen size, orientation, safe areas, and content changes.

blocks.code:
label.translatesAutoresizingMaskIntoConstraints = false
NSLayoutConstraint.activate([
    label.centerXAnchor.constraint(equalTo: view.centerXAnchor),
    label.centerYAnchor.constraint(equalTo: view.centerYAnchor)
])
blocks.title: Basic Auto Layout Constraints

blocks.textContent: The mental model is relational, not absolute. You are usually saying things like 'pin this edge', 'match this width', or 'keep this spacing', which makes the interface more resilient than hard-coded frame math when environments change.

When building views in code, translatesAutoresizingMaskIntoConstraints must usually be false. Otherwise, UIKit generates constraints from the autoresizing mask, which often conflicts with the constraints you intended to express explicitly.

Auto Layout becomes much easier once you stop treating it as magic and instead see it as a system for expressing consistent relationships. Most layout bugs come from missing information, contradictory information, or an unclear view hierarchy.

topics.title: NSLayoutConstraint
topics.summary: Represents a single relationship that Auto Layout uses to solve a view hierarchy.

blocks.textContent: NSLayoutConstraint is the concrete object that expresses one layout relationship. It can connect anchors, define a constant size, enforce proportions, or express priorities that help UIKit decide which constraints may bend under pressure.

blocks.code:
let widthConstraint = cardView.widthAnchor.constraint(equalToConstant: 240)
widthConstraint.isActive = true
blocks.title: Creating a Constraint

blocks.textContent: Priority is one of the most useful parts of the API. Required constraints must hold, while lower-priority constraints express preferences that the engine may relax when the layout would otherwise become unsatisfiable.

blocks.title: Setting Constraint Priority

blocks.textContent: Thinking at the level of individual constraints is what eventually makes Auto Layout debuggable. Once you can read a layout as a set of explicit relationships, warnings and ambiguity become much easier to reason about.

topics.title: Safe Area
topics.summary: Defines the portion of the interface that avoids system bars, cutouts, and other obstructing elements.

blocks.textContent: The safe area is the region of a view where content is less likely to be obscured by bars, notches, indicators, or other system interface elements. It is one of UIKit's main tools for making layouts adapt cleanly across devices.

blocks.code:
NSLayoutConstraint.activate([
    contentView.topAnchor.constraint(equalTo: view.safeAreaLayoutGuide.topAnchor),
    contentView.leadingAnchor.constraint(equalTo: view.safeAreaLayoutGuide.leadingAnchor),
    contentView.trailingAnchor.constraint(equalTo: view.safeAreaLayoutGuide.trailingAnchor)
])
blocks.title: Constraining to the Safe Area

blocks.textContent: In practice, the safe area is less about a specific inset value and more about intent. It tells UIKit which content should respect system chrome automatically and which content, such as backgrounds or immersive media, may extend underneath it.

Scroll views, navigation bars, tab bars, and presented controllers all interact with the safe area. Once you understand that relationship, many edge-inset and clipping issues stop feeling mysterious and start looking structural.

A common production pattern is letting decorative backgrounds ignore the safe area while keeping interactive or readable content inside it. That split usually gives the interface a fuller visual presence without hurting usability.

topics.title: UILayoutGuide
topics.summary: Provides a non-rendering layout region you can constrain against.

blocks.textContent: UILayoutGuide is a layout-only object with anchors but no visual output. It is useful when you need structure in the constraint system without introducing an extra wrapper view just to hold space or define a region.

blocks.code:
let guide = UILayoutGuide()
view.addLayoutGuide(guide)

NSLayoutConstraint.activate([
    guide.topAnchor.constraint(equalTo: view.safeAreaLayoutGuide.topAnchor),
    guide.leadingAnchor.constraint(equalTo: view.leadingAnchor, constant: 24),
    guide.trailingAnchor.constraint(equalTo: view.trailingAnchor, constant: -24)
])
blocks.title: Using a Layout Guide

blocks.textContent: The main advantage is conceptual cleanliness. If you only need anchors, not a rendered surface, a layout guide keeps the hierarchy simpler and makes it clearer that the region exists for geometry rather than presentation.

Many familiar UIKit layout surfaces are guides already, such as safeAreaLayoutGuide, contentLayoutGuide, and frameLayoutGuide. Learning UILayoutGuide directly helps those APIs feel like part of one coherent system instead of unrelated exceptions.

Layout guides are especially useful in complex screens where extra wrapper views would otherwise multiply purely for spacing or grouping reasons. Used well, they reduce hierarchy noise without reducing layout clarity.

topics.title: Intrinsic Content Size
topics.summary: Describes the natural size a view prefers based on its content.

blocks.textContent: Intrinsic content size is a view's natural size derived from what it displays. Labels, buttons, images, and many other UIKit views can tell Auto Layout how large they want to be without needing explicit width and height constraints in every case.

This matters because not every dimension should be hard-coded. If a label knows its natural width and height from its text and font, the layout can often stay more flexible and more semantically correct by leaning on that information.

blocks.title: A View with Intrinsic Content Size

blocks.textContent: Custom views can also participate by overriding intrinsicContentSize when they have a meaningful natural size. That is often cleaner than forcing callers to provide explicit constraints for something the view can determine itself.

Many Auto Layout misunderstandings clear up once you ask which dimensions are intrinsic and which must be constrained externally. That distinction explains why some layouts 'just work' while others remain ambiguous until more information is added.

topics.title: Content Hugging and Compression Resistance
topics.summary: Helps Auto Layout decide which views should stretch or shrink first when space changes.

blocks.textContent: Content hugging and compression resistance are priority-based signals that help Auto Layout resolve competition for space. They answer two different questions: which view resists growing larger, and which view resists being made smaller.

These values become important when multiple views with intrinsic sizes sit in the same axis, such as a title label next to a button or an icon next to a text stack. Without priorities, the engine may not know which element should yield first.

blocks.code:
titleLabel.setContentHuggingPriority(.defaultLow, for: .horizontal)
actionButton.setContentHuggingPriority(.defaultHigh, for: .horizontal)

titleLabel.setContentCompressionResistancePriority(.defaultLow, for: .horizontal)
blocks.title: Adjusting Layout Priorities

blocks.textContent: This is one of the most practical Auto Layout tools because many seemingly mysterious stretching or truncation bugs are really just missing hugging or compression guidance. Once the priorities reflect intent, the layout often becomes stable immediately.

In production UI, these priorities are often the difference between a layout that handles localization and Dynamic Type gracefully and one that breaks the moment text becomes longer than the original design expected.

topics.title: Trait Collections
topics.summary: Describe environment characteristics such as size classes and appearance that influence adaptive behavior.

blocks.textContent: Trait collections describe the current interface environment, including size classes, display scale, user interface style, and other contextual values. They are one of UIKit's main mechanisms for making interfaces adapt beyond simple screen dimensions.

Horizontal and vertical size classes are the most visible part of the system because they let the app reason in broad layout categories rather than in fragile device-specific branches. That is usually the right level of abstraction for adaptive UI.

blocks.title: Adapting to Size Class

blocks.textContent: Traits can change during the life of a controller, especially on iPad, multitasking environments, and appearance transitions. Good adaptive code reacts to those changes rather than assuming the launch environment will never shift.

Trait collections are most useful when they influence structure, spacing, or presentation strategy, not when they become a reason to fork entire interfaces unnecessarily. The best adaptive UIKit code changes what matters while preserving a stable mental model.

topics.title: Push Navigation
topics.summary: Moves deeper into a navigation stack by pushing a new view controller on top.

blocks.textContent: Push navigation is the standard UIKit pattern for moving deeper into a hierarchical flow. It depends on a navigation controller and works by placing a new view controller on top of the existing stack.

The important mental model is continuity. The next screen feels like a more detailed layer of the current context rather than a separate mode, which is why push navigation fits list-detail and drill-down experiences so naturally.

blocks.code:
let detailViewController = DetailViewController()
navigationController?.pushViewController(detailViewController, animated: true)
blocks.title: Pushing a Screen

blocks.textContent: Because the previous screen remains in the stack, the system can provide back navigation, state continuity, and a clearer sense of place. That persistence is one of the main reasons push flows feel reliable and native on iOS.

Push navigation works best when the destination is conceptually downstream from the current screen. If the next interface is really a separate task or mode, modal presentation is often the more honest interaction model.

topics.title: Modal Presentation
topics.summary: Presents a view controller above the current interface for a separate task or transient flow.

blocks.textContent: Modal presentation shows a view controller on top of the current interface instead of pushing it into the existing hierarchy. It is the right fit when the new screen represents a distinct task, interruption, or focused flow that should stand apart from the current stack.

blocks.code:
let composeViewController = ComposeViewController()
present(composeViewController, animated: true)
blocks.title: Presenting a Modal Screen

blocks.textContent: UIKit supports several modal styles such as fullScreen, pageSheet, and formSheet. The style affects how separate the presented interface feels from the underlying content and how adaptively it behaves across devices.

blocks.code:
composeViewController.modalPresentationStyle = .pageSheet
present(composeViewController, animated: true)
blocks.title: Choosing a Presentation Style

blocks.textContent: A modal flow also needs a clear dismissal path, whether that is a done button, cancel action, swipe gesture, or completion state. The presentation feels polished when entry and exit are equally deliberate.

The real design question is whether the new screen belongs inside the current hierarchy or temporarily sits above it. That distinction usually matters more than the exact animation or presentation style you choose.

topics.title: UIAlertController
topics.summary: Presents alerts and action sheets to communicate important information or request a choice.

blocks.textContent: UIAlertController is UIKit's standard API for alerts and action sheets. It lets the app interrupt the user briefly to confirm an action, present a small set of choices, or communicate important state that needs immediate attention.

blocks.code:
let alert = UIAlertController(title: "Delete File", message: "This action cannot be undone.", preferredStyle: .alert)
alert.addAction(UIAlertAction(title: "Cancel", style: .cancel))
alert.addAction(UIAlertAction(title: "Delete", style: .destructive))
present(alert, animated: true)
blocks.title: Basic Alert

blocks.textContent: The two main modes are alert and action sheet. Alerts are more interruptive and often used for confirmation or errors, while action sheets are better for presenting a set of related actions anchored in the current context.

Action styles such as default, cancel, and destructive carry semantic meaning as well as visual treatment. Good alerts are usually defined less by their wording alone and more by whether the available actions communicate consequence clearly.
Alerts should stay focused and rare. Once they are used for routine navigation or every minor message, they stop feeling important and start slowing the interface down unnecessarily.

topics.title: UIContextMenuInteraction
topics.summary: Adds context menus with previews and actions to interface elements.

blocks.textContent: UIContextMenuInteraction brings context menus to UIKit views. It lets users access secondary actions, previews, and richer interaction affordances without giving each action permanent space in the main interface.

This interaction is especially useful for overflow actions and item-specific commands that are real but not primary. It keeps the screen cleaner while still making advanced capabilities discoverable through long press or right click.

blocks.code:
let interaction = UIContextMenuInteraction(delegate: self)
imageView.addInteraction(interaction)
blocks.title: Adding a Context Menu Interaction

blocks.textContent: The delegate provides a menu configuration, optional preview, and actions. That separation keeps the view lightweight while the menu content remains driven by model state and current context.

blocks.title: Building a Context Menu

blocks.textContent: Context menus work best for secondary actions, not for core flows that users must discover immediately. If an action is central to the experience, it usually deserves visible placement rather than depending on a hidden gesture.

topics.title: UIGestureRecognizer
topics.summary: The base class for recognizing common touch-based interaction patterns.

blocks.textContent: UIGestureRecognizer is UIKit's abstraction for recognizing interaction patterns such as taps, pans, pinches, long presses, drags, and drops. Instead of tracking raw touches manually, you attach recognizers and let the system interpret gesture intent.

Gesture recognizers also have state, which is important because many interactions are not instantaneous. Began, changed, ended, failed, and cancelled make it possible to model interactions that evolve over time rather than just fire once.

blocks.code:
let tapRecognizer = UITapGestureRecognizer(target: self, action: #selector(handleTap))
view.addGestureRecognizer(tapRecognizer)
blocks.title: Adding a Gesture Recognizer

blocks.textContent: The API becomes especially interesting when gestures interact with each other or with controls and scroll views. Delegation and failure requirements let you coordinate those relationships instead of forcing every view to fight for touches independently.

Gesture recognizers are powerful because they separate interaction recognition from view rendering. Used carefully, they make advanced interaction feel modular instead of welded into every custom view class.

topics.title: UITapGestureRecognizer
topics.summary: Recognizes single or multiple taps on a view.

blocks.textContent: UITapGestureRecognizer detects tap gestures without requiring low-level touch handling. It is often used to make non-control views interactive, dismiss keyboards, trigger previews, or respond to image and card taps in a lightweight way.

blocks.title: Basic Tap Gesture

blocks.textContent: numberOfTapsRequired and numberOfTouchesRequired let the recognizer express slightly richer intent, though most production uses stay simple because uncommon tap patterns can become harder for users to discover and perform reliably.

If a view is meant to behave like a button most of the time, a real control is often the better choice. Tap recognizers are strongest when the interaction is attached to content or composition that is not naturally modeled as a control subclass.

topics.title: UILongPressGestureRecognizer
topics.summary: Recognizes a press held for a minimum duration before triggering.

blocks.textContent: UILongPressGestureRecognizer recognizes a sustained press rather than an immediate tap. It is commonly used for context menus, reordering affordances, selection modes, and interactions where the app wants a stronger signal of intent than a quick touch provides.

Unlike a simple tap, a long press usually matters at the moment it begins and may continue to matter while the finger remains down. Checking the recognizer state is therefore part of using it correctly.

blocks.code:
let recognizer = UILongPressGestureRecognizer(target: self, action: #selector(handleLongPress(_:)))
view.addGestureRecognizer(recognizer)
blocks.title: Basic Long Press Gesture

blocks.textContent: Long press is useful for secondary or expert interactions, but it should not hide critical functionality entirely. If the only path to an important action depends on a non-obvious gesture, discoverability usually suffers.

topics.title: UIPanGestureRecognizer
topics.summary: Tracks dragging movement and translation across a view.

blocks.textContent: UIPanGestureRecognizer tracks continuous dragging movement. It is the foundation for interactions such as dragging cards, scrubbing custom controls, moving views, and building gesture-driven transitions or bottom sheets.

The most useful values are translation and velocity. Translation tells you how far the finger has moved relative to a view, while velocity helps infer momentum and decide how an interaction should complete.

blocks.code:
let panRecognizer = UIPanGestureRecognizer(target: self, action: #selector(handlePan(_:)))
cardView.addGestureRecognizer(panRecognizer)
blocks.title: Basic Pan Gesture

blocks.textContent: Pan gestures are powerful, but they can also conflict with scroll views and system gestures quickly. The best implementations are explicit about which axis matters, when the gesture should begin, and how it should coexist with surrounding interactions.

topics.title: UIDragInteraction
topics.summary: Enables drag operations that let people lift and move items from a view.

blocks.textContent: UIDragInteraction adds drag support to UIKit views. It lets the user begin dragging data, preview the lifted content, and move that content within the app or into other compatible apps depending on the context.

The drag system is fundamentally about data representation. The visual lift matters, but the real payload comes from NSItemProvider-backed items that describe what the drag can offer to a drop destination.

blocks.title: Adding Drag Interaction

blocks.textContent: Drag interaction feels best when the content is clearly movable and the result is meaningful, such as dragging files, photos, tags, or list items. If the operation is abstract or hidden, users are unlikely to discover it confidently.

topics.title: UIDropInteraction
topics.summary: Enables a view to accept dropped content from drags within the app or from other apps.

blocks.textContent: UIDropInteraction is the counterpart to drag interaction. It allows a view to declare that it can receive dropped data, evaluate what is being offered, and decide how the interface should react as the drag enters, updates, and completes.

A drop is more than a final event. The delegate can inspect the session, propose how the drop should be handled, update visual feedback, and then load the dropped data asynchronously once the operation is accepted.

blocks.code:
let dropInteraction = UIDropInteraction(delegate: self)
canvasView.addInteraction(dropInteraction)
blocks.title: Adding Drop Interaction

blocks.textContent: Drop support is strongest when the target surface and resulting behavior are visually obvious. If the app accepts drops but offers little feedback about where or why, the interaction tends to feel fragile instead of empowering.

topics.title: UIColor
topics.summary: Defines colors for text, fills, tints, and other visual elements in UIKit.

blocks.textContent: UIColor is UIKit's color type. It appears everywhere in interface work, from backgrounds and tinting to text, separators, shadows, and layered visual states.

In production UI, semantic system colors such as label, secondaryLabel, systemBackground, and separator are often more important than custom RGB values because they adapt automatically to light mode, dark mode, contrast settings, and platform conventions.

blocks.code:
view.backgroundColor = .systemBackground
titleLabel.textColor = .label
accentView.tintColor = .systemBlue
blocks.title: Using Semantic Colors

blocks.textContent: UIColor can also be dynamic, resolving differently depending on the current trait collection. That capability is part of what makes modern UIKit styling adaptive rather than locked to one visual environment.

The best color decisions in UIKit usually come from choosing the right semantic role first and custom branding second. That approach keeps the interface more resilient as appearance settings and contexts evolve.

topics.title: UIFont
topics.summary: Defines typefaces, text styles, and font metrics for UIKit text rendering.

blocks.textContent: UIFont is UIKit's font type. It shapes hierarchy, tone, readability, and how comfortably the interface adapts to different content sizes and accessibility settings.

Preferred text styles such as body, headline, title, and caption are usually the most practical starting point because they align typography with Dynamic Type and system conventions instead of freezing size decisions too early.

blocks.code:
titleLabel.font = .preferredFont(forTextStyle: .headline)
captionLabel.font = .preferredFont(forTextStyle: .caption1)
blocks.title: Using Preferred Text Styles

blocks.textContent: Custom fonts still have a place, but they work best when paired with UIFontMetrics so sizing can scale appropriately. Without that step, typography often looks polished in one environment and brittle in another.

Typography quality in UIKit is usually less about decorative choices and more about consistent hierarchy, legibility, and responsiveness to content size changes. UIFont is where all of those concerns meet.

topics.title: UIImage
topics.summary: Represents image data for assets, symbols, and rendered visual content in UIKit.

blocks.textContent: UIImage is UIKit's image object. It represents bitmap content, symbol-based images, and other visual resources that views such as UIImageView, UIButton, and menu actions can render.

Images may come from the asset catalog, SF Symbols, generated drawing, data loaded from disk or network, or rendering pipelines elsewhere in the app. UIImage is the common object that lets all those sources flow into UIKit presentation APIs.

blocks.code:
let avatar = UIImage(named: "avatar")
let heart = UIImage(systemName: "heart.fill")
blocks.title: Asset and Symbol Images

blocks.textContent: Rendering mode matters, especially for template images and symbols. Some images are meant to preserve their original colors, while others are designed to inherit tint from the surrounding interface.

UIImage itself does not decide layout or interaction, but it strongly influences both through the views that render it. Understanding the image object helps clarify where data ends and visual presentation begins.

topics.title: CALayer
topics.summary: Provides the underlying rendering and visual styling surface for UIKit views.

blocks.textContent: Every UIView has a backing CALayer. The layer handles much of the rendering-related behavior that developers use for styling and animation, including corner radius, border, shadow, masks, transforms, and compositing details.

blocks.code:
cardView.layer.cornerRadius = 16
cardView.layer.borderWidth = 1
cardView.layer.borderColor = UIColor.separator.cgColor
cardView.layer.shadowOpacity = 0.15
blocks.title: Styling a View Through Its Layer

blocks.textContent: CALayer matters because many UIKit appearance decisions are really layer decisions. Once you notice that distinction, effects like clipping, shadow behavior, and transform-based animation become easier to reason about.

Layers are powerful, but they are still part of a rendering system with tradeoffs. Deep shadow stacks, offscreen rendering triggers, and masking-heavy designs can all affect performance if used carelessly at scale.

topics.title: UIButton.Configuration
topics.summary: Defines modern button content and appearance using a configuration-based API.

blocks.textContent: UIButton.Configuration is the modern styling API for buttons in UIKit. It packages content, padding, image placement, subtitle, background style, and overall visual intent into one configuration object.

This matters because older UIButton styling often spread across several setters and state branches. Configuration moves the API toward a more declarative shape, making button setup easier to read and more consistent.

blocks.title: Using a Button Configuration

blocks.textContent: Configuration is especially useful when buttons need richer structure without becoming custom views. It keeps the button in UIKit's control model while still allowing more expressive, modern visual design.

topics.title: Dynamic Type
topics.summary: Lets text and interface typography scale according to the user's preferred content size.

blocks.textContent: Dynamic Type is the system's typography scaling feature for accessibility and user preference. In UIKit, supporting it well means more than picking preferred fonts; it also means ensuring layouts, spacing, and truncation behavior stay resilient as content grows.

blocks.code:
titleLabel.font = .preferredFont(forTextStyle: .body)
titleLabel.adjustsFontForContentSizeCategory = true
blocks.title: Supporting Dynamic Type

blocks.textContent: The real challenge is usually layout, not font assignment. A screen that looks balanced at the default text size may break quickly if hugging, compression resistance, line count, and spacing were never designed to adapt.

Dynamic Type support is often one of the clearest signals of interface maturity. When UIKit screens respect content size changes well, the whole app tends to feel more robust, inclusive, and system-native.

topics.title: SF Symbols in UIKit
topics.summary: Uses Apple's symbol-based icon system in UIKit controls, images, and menus.

blocks.textContent: SF Symbols are Apple's system icon set, and UIKit integrates with them naturally through UIImage and related configuration APIs. They are especially useful because they align with typography, support semantic scaling, and match platform visual language by default.

blocks.title: Using an SF Symbol

blocks.textContent: Symbol configuration controls scale, weight, point size, palette, and hierarchical appearance. That flexibility makes symbols feel much closer to typographic elements than to fixed bitmap assets.

blocks.code:
let config = UIImage.SymbolConfiguration(pointSize: 20, weight: .semibold)
let image = UIImage(systemName: "paperplane.fill", withConfiguration: config)
blocks.title: Configuring a Symbol Image

blocks.textContent: SF Symbols are usually the best default for action icons and lightweight status imagery in UIKit. They reduce asset management overhead while improving consistency with the rest of the system interface.

topics.title: UIView.animate
topics.summary: Animates view property changes over time using UIKit's simplest high-level animation API.

blocks.textContent: UIView.animate is the standard entry point for simple UIKit animations. You change animatable properties inside the animation block, and UIKit interpolates the transition over the specified duration.

blocks.title: Basic UIView Animation

blocks.textContent: This API works best for straightforward changes to animatable properties such as frame, bounds, center, alpha, transform, background color, and some layer-backed styling. It is usually the fastest path to motion that already feels native enough for many screens.

Completion handlers matter because many animations are really part of a larger state change. The visual transition may finish in 300 milliseconds, but the surrounding interface logic often depends on knowing exactly when that happened.

blocks.title: Animation with Completion

blocks.textContent: UIKit animation quality is often less about complexity and more about restraint. Small changes to opacity, transform, and layout can make an interface feel far more responsive without needing a custom animation system.

topics.title: Spring Animations
topics.summary: Use spring timing to create motion that feels more physical, responsive, and natural.

blocks.textContent: Spring animations give UIKit motion a more physical feel by modeling damping and initial velocity. They are especially effective when views enter, settle, expand, collapse, or respond to direct user interaction.

blocks.title: Basic Spring Animation

blocks.textContent: Damping controls how much the motion oscillates before settling, while initial velocity influences how strongly the movement starts. Together, those values determine whether the result feels gentle, snappy, or overly theatrical.

Springs are most effective when they reinforce interface meaning rather than calling attention to themselves. A subtle spring can make a card feel tappable or a panel feel grounded, while an exaggerated one quickly turns into noise.

topics.title: UIViewPropertyAnimator
topics.summary: Creates interruptible and interactive animations with explicit timing and state control.

blocks.textContent: UIViewPropertyAnimator gives UIKit a more controllable animation model than the basic animate methods. It is designed for animations that may need to pause, reverse, scrub interactively, or be coordinated more explicitly over time.

blocks.title: Basic Property Animator

blocks.textContent: The major advantage is interruptibility. Instead of firing and forgetting an animation, you can hold onto the animator, inspect or update its progress, and connect it to gesture-driven interactions in a much cleaner way.

blocks.code:
animator.pauseAnimation()
animator.fractionComplete = 0.5
blocks.title: Controlling Animation Progress

blocks.textContent: This API is especially valuable for custom drawers, cards, bottom sheets, and gesture-coupled transitions. Once motion needs to follow the user's hand rather than just run on a timer, property animator usually becomes the better tool.

topics.title: View Controller Transitions
topics.summary: Customize how view controllers are presented, dismissed, pushed, and animated between states.

blocks.textContent: View controller transitions define how UIKit moves between screens beyond the default push and modal animations. They matter when the app wants motion that better matches the structure or emotional tone of the destination.

Custom transitions are delegate-driven. UIKit asks for animator and, optionally, interaction controller objects that describe how the transition should run and whether the user can control it interactively.

blocks.code:
presentedViewController.modalPresentationStyle = .custom
presentedViewController.transitioningDelegate = self
blocks.title: Starting a Custom Transition

blocks.textContent: Custom transitions are worth it when motion adds clarity, continuity, or a stronger sense of spatial relationship between screens. If the transition exists only to look different, the maintenance cost often outweighs the payoff.

topics.title: Target-Action
topics.summary: Connects interactive UIKit controls to code that responds when specific events occur.

blocks.textContent: Target-action is one of UIKit's core interaction patterns. A control sends an event, and a target object handles that event through a selector-based action method.

blocks.code: button.addTarget(self, action: #selector(saveTapped), for: .touchUpInside)
blocks.title: Connecting a Control Event

blocks.textContent: The pattern matters because it keeps controls generic while still allowing the app to decide what each event means. UIKit uses it broadly across buttons, switches, sliders, text fields, refresh controls, and many other interactive components.

Even in modern UIKit, target-action remains foundational because it is lightweight, explicit, and deeply integrated into the framework's control model. Understanding it also makes newer closure-based wrappers easier to reason about.

topics.title: Delegates and Data Sources
topics.summary: Separate UIKit infrastructure from app-specific behavior and data through protocol-based collaboration.

blocks.textContent: Delegates and data sources are among UIKit's most important architectural patterns. The framework owns generic UI infrastructure, while your objects provide the app-specific behavior, decisions, and content through protocol conformance.
The distinction is conceptual as much as technical. A data source answers what content exists, while a delegate usually answers how the component should behave or respond during user interaction.

blocks.code:
tableView.dataSource = self
tableView.delegate = self
textField.delegate = self
blocks.title: Assigning Delegates and Data Sources

blocks.textContent: This protocol-driven style is a large part of why UIKit stays modular. The tradeoff is that it can spread behavior across several callbacks, so clean organization and clear ownership become especially important in larger screens.

topics.title: NotificationCenter with UIKit
topics.summary: Observes app and system events to coordinate UIKit behavior across separate parts of the interface.

blocks.textContent: NotificationCenter is a broadcast-based event system that UIKit code often uses for cross-cutting concerns. Keyboard changes, app lifecycle events, and custom app-wide state changes are common examples.

blocks.code:
NotificationCenter.default.addObserver(
    self,
    selector: #selector(keyboardWillShow(_:)),
    name: UIResponder.keyboardWillShowNotification,
    object: nil
)
blocks.title: Observing a UIKit Notification

blocks.textContent: The system is useful when the sender and receiver should remain loosely coupled. That is valuable, but it also means notification-driven code can become harder to trace if used as a default communication style for everything.

NotificationCenter is strongest for broad environmental changes and weaker for local screen logic that already has a direct ownership path. The right boundary usually keeps it as infrastructure, not as the main architecture of the app.

topics.title: Combine with UIKit
topics.summary: Use reactive streams to observe events, transform values, and drive UIKit updates.

blocks.textContent: Combine can sit comfortably alongside UIKit even though UIKit itself is largely delegate- and target-action-driven. It becomes useful when screen logic benefits from stream composition, debouncing, transformation, cancellation, or shared reactive state.

The biggest practical rule is that UI updates still belong on the main thread. Combine can shape the data flow, but UIKit remains imperative at the rendering boundary where values become visible interface changes.

blocks.code:
publisher
    .receive(on: DispatchQueue.main)
    .sink { [weak self] value in
        self?.titleLabel.text = value
    }
    .store(in: &cancellables)
blocks.title: Driving UIKit from a Publisher

blocks.textContent: Combine is most helpful when it simplifies state flow, not when it adds another abstraction layer without reducing complexity. In UIKit codebases, the best integrations usually keep the view update boundary very explicit.

topics.title: SwiftUI Interoperability
topics.summary: Bridge UIKit and SwiftUI so screens and components from both frameworks can coexist intentionally.

blocks.textContent: UIKit and SwiftUI are designed to coexist. Interoperability matters because many real apps mix legacy UIKit infrastructure with newer SwiftUI surfaces rather than rewriting everything at once.

The two most common bridges are UIHostingController for presenting SwiftUI from UIKit and UIViewRepresentable or UIViewControllerRepresentable for embedding UIKit inside SwiftUI. Those APIs define where each framework becomes the host and where the other becomes the guest.

blocks.code:
let hostingController = UIHostingController(rootView: ProfileView())
navigationController?.pushViewController(hostingController, animated: true)
blocks.title: Presenting SwiftUI from UIKit

blocks.textContent: Interoperability works best when the boundary is intentional. If state ownership, navigation responsibility, or lifecycle expectations are unclear at the framework seam, the integration quickly becomes harder to maintain than either framework alone.
